text:
EDMUND. I possess no less claim to our father's blood than you, though I grant that age has placed you before me.
ALARIC. What insolence is this?
EDMUND. Brother, do not mistake firmness for insolence.
ALARIC. Will you challenge me in my own house?
EDMUND. I challenge only injustice. I am the son of Sir Geoffrey, as surely as you are. Whoever speaks dishonourably of his sons condemns the father who raised them.
MARTIN. [Stepping forward.] Good masters, remember your father's memory. Let peace stand between you.
ALARIC. Release me.
EDMUND. Not until you hear me. Our father left instructions that I should be brought up according to my station. Instead, I have been neglected, denied learning and exercise, while you enjoyed every advantage. Yet I feel my father's spirit within me, and I will not endure this treatment longer. Give me the education proper to my birth, or grant me the inheritance left in my name. With it I shall seek my own fortune.
ALARIC. And when that money is gone, what then? Will you wander the roads begging? Very well. You shall have something. Now leave me.
EDMUND. I ask only what is mine by right.
ALARIC. Away with him. And take this old servant too.
MARTIN. So "old servant" is all I have earned? My years were spent faithfully in this house. Your father would never have spoken so coldly.
Exeunt Edmund and Martin.
ALARIC. So. The boy grows bold. I must cure that ambition before it spreads further. Ho there! Stephen!
Enter Stephen.
STEPHEN. My lord?
ALARIC. Was not Victor, the duke's champion, seeking audience with me?
STEPHEN. He waits at the gate and asks leave to enter.
ALARIC. Admit him.
Exit Stephen.
The timing serves me well. Tomorrow's contest may solve more than one problem.
Enter Victor.
VICTOR. Good day, my lord.
ALARIC. Welcome, Victor. What news travels from court?
VICTOR. Little beyond what everyone already knows. The elder duke remains in exile, and those loyal to him have followed him into the northern woods. Their estates now enrich the younger duke.
ALARIC. And his daughter? Did she accompany him?
VICTOR. No. The duke's niece would not be separated from her cousin. Their affection is so strong that neither would remain without the other. Both ladies continue at court and are greatly admired.
ALARIC. Where does the exiled duke now reside?
VICTOR. In the Greenwood Forest, they say. Many gentlemen join him there. They hunt, feast, and pass their days with remarkable freedom.
ALARIC. You compete tomorrow before the duke?
VICTOR. I do. That is partly why I came. I have learned that your younger brother intends to enter the contest against me under disguise.
ALARIC. Indeed?
VICTOR. He is young, and I would regret harming him. Yet in a public match I cannot spare him without dishonouring myself. If he steps into the ring, he risks injury. I thought it proper to warn you.
ALARIC. Your concern does you credit. I have already attempted to dissuade him, but he refuses all advice. He is stubborn beyond reason.
VICTOR. Then whatever follows will be of his own choosing.
ALARIC. So it will.

EDMUND. Our father's blood runs in me no less than in you, though I admit the privilege of birth has placed you first.
ALARIC. Bold words.
EDMUND. Necessary ones.
ALARIC. Do you mean to threaten me?
EDMUND. No. I mean to speak plainly. I am Sir Geoffrey's son as surely as you are, and I will not stand silent while I am treated as though I were less.
MARTIN. [Moving between them.] Gentlemen, for your father's sake, let this quarrel end.
ALARIC. Stand aside.
EDMUND. Not yet. You must hear me first. Our father wished me educated as a gentleman. Instead, I have been kept idle and ignorant while every opportunity was reserved for you. I have tolerated it long enough. Either provide what was promised me, or release the inheritance my father left. I will take my chances elsewhere.
ALARIC. And squander it within a year?
EDMUND. That risk belongs to me.
ALARIC. Very well. I tire of arguing. Leave me.
EDMUND. I seek only fairness.
ALARIC. Go, then—and take your aged companion with you.
MARTIN. A poor reward for faithful service. Your father had kinder words for those who served him.
They exit.
ALARIC. The boy has grown dangerous. Ambition is easier crushed early than late. Stephen!
Enter Stephen.
STEPHEN. My lord?
ALARIC. Has Victor arrived?
STEPHEN. He waits outside.
ALARIC. Bring him in.
Stephen exits.
Interesting. Tomorrow's tournament may spare me future trouble.
Victor enters.
VICTOR. My lord.
ALARIC. Victor. What news from court?
VICTOR. Nothing new, only familiar stories. The elder duke remains in exile. Several loyal nobles followed him, leaving their lands behind. Those estates now belong to the younger duke.
ALARIC. And the duke's daughter?
VICTOR. Still at court. She and her cousin are inseparable. One would not leave the other.
ALARIC. Where has the exiled duke settled?
VICTOR. Deep in the Greenwood Forest. More young nobles join him each week. By all accounts they live freely there.
ALARIC. You wrestle before the duke tomorrow?
VICTOR. Yes, and that brings me here. Word has reached me that your brother plans to challenge me.
ALARIC. Orlando—always eager to prove himself.
VICTOR. I bear him no ill will. Yet once the match begins, I cannot show mercy without losing reputation. If he enters the contest, he may leave it injured.
ALARIC. I appreciate your warning. I have tried to change his mind, but he listens to no one.
VICTOR. Then the responsibility is his.
ALARIC. Entirely his.
Victor nods. Alaric watches him go, already turning the matter to his own advantage.

SCENE. A market square before the city gates.
Alarums and shouting within. Enter ROWE, a rebel captain, with his followers.
ROWE. Come, friends, the bell hath rung for liberty. Why stand ye idle? Some to the guildhall, and some unto the custom house. Let no account-books survive this day. Down with the ledgers that have weighed men's backs more heavily than iron.
MARTIN. Captain, I crave a favor of your grace.
ROWE. Grace? Thou hast made me nobler with that word than any king could do. Speak thy desire.
MARTIN. Only that no tax be laid save by the consent of those that pay it.
POTTER. [Aside.] Then shall the treasury be as lean as a winter hound.
COOPER. [Aside.] Ay, and the clerks will learn to reckon upon their fingers.
ROWE. It is resolved. Henceforth no parchment shall command where honest speech may suffice. Let every grievance be heard in the open square.
POTTER. Then we shall have long judgments, for every man loves the sound of his own complaint.
ROWE. Better that than secret decrees hatched behind locked doors. From this hour, the city belongs to its people.
Enter a MESSENGER in haste.
MESSENGER. Captain, a capture! A capture! Here is Lord Varney, chief councillor to the Regent, who gathered the levies and doubled the tolls upon the roads.
Enter HUGH with LORD VARNEY guarded.
ROWE. Well found. Bring him forth. My lord, you stand where rank affords no shelter. What answer make you to those whose harvests paid for your feasts? What say you to widows whose sons were taken for wars they never understood?
VARNEY. I shall answer what is true, if truth be permitted here.
ROWE. Truth? We have heard much of truth from men in velvet. Yet the granaries emptied while your household prospered. The roads decayed while new statues rose before the palace. The poor were taught patience, and the rich exemption.
HUGH. Aye, and every year another levy.
MARTIN. And another promise.
ROWE. It shall be proved that you surrounded yourself with scholars and secretaries who measured the kingdom by columns of figures and knew not the faces of the people whose lives those figures marked. You built offices, commissions, and courts. You sent summonses to men who could scarce write their names. When they failed to answer in the language of lawyers, they were fined. When they could not pay, they were imprisoned.
VARNEY. The law must be administered.
COOPER. Administered? Like physic to a healthy man till he dies of the cure.
ROWE. Tell us, my lord, how many days have you labored with your own hands?
VARNEY. Few, for my office required another service.
ROWE. Another service indeed. Your horse wears silver trappings while laborers patch their boots with rope.
MARTIN. And some go barefoot altogether.
VARNEY. Good people, hear me. You judge by appearances and by rumors sharpened in the telling. The tolls maintained bridges. The levies defended borders. The courts restrained thieves and protected contracts. A kingdom is not governed by wishes but by difficult choices.
ROWE. Fine words come easily to practiced tongues.
VARNEY. Then weigh deeds instead. When famine threatened the northern counties, whose stores were opened? Mine. When floods swept the valley farms, whose treasury paid for repairs? Mine. When merchants sought to abandon the harbor, who persuaded them to remain? I did, for the benefit of the city and its trade.
HUGH. He speaks smoothly.
MARTIN. Smoothly enough to oil a gallows rope.
VARNEY. If I have erred, I am a man and not a saint. Yet consider what follows if every record burns and every magistrate flees. Debts will be disputed, boundaries forgotten, inheritances contested. Neighbors will quarrel where judges once decided. The strongest voice will become the law.
ROWE. And what is the law now, but the strongest voice made permanent?
VARNEY. No. The law, imperfect though it be, is a shield placed between power and vengeance. Break it utterly, and you may cheer today, but tomorrow you will search among the ruins for what protected you.
A murmur passes through the crowd.
POTTER. There is reason in some part of what he says.
COOPER. And guilt in another.
ROWE. Silence. My lord, you plead well for one whose cause stands upon such uncertain ground.
VARNEY. I plead not for myself alone. I plead for the city. Anger may overthrow a government in an afternoon; wisdom is required to build another.
ROWE. We shall determine whether wisdom or anger rules this day. Guard him well. The people shall hear all charges before sentence is pronounced.
HUGH. Come, my lord.
VARNEY. I am ready. May judgment be fairer than rumor.

SCENE. A market square before the city gates.
Noise within. Bells ringing. Enter ROWE with a band of townsmen.
ROWE. Why linger here? The city's waking at last. Off with you—some to the guildhall, some to the custom house. If you find a ledger, throw it in the fire. Those books have burdened more shoulders than any packhorse.
MARTIN. Captain, I would ask a favor.
ROWE. A favor? Why, man, you make me sound a prince. Speak it.
MARTIN. Only this: let no man be taxed unless he consents to it.
POTTER. [Aside.] Then the treasury shall soon be begging its supper.
COOPER. [Aside.] And the clerks may learn a trade at last.
ROWE. Good. Let it be so. Enough of sealed orders and hidden accounts. If a man has a grievance, let him bring it before his neighbors and speak it plainly.
POTTER. There'll be no end to speaking, then.
ROWE. Better too much speech than too much silence.
A MESSENGER rushes in.
MESSENGER. Captain! We have him. Lord Varney is taken.
Enter HUGH with LORD VARNEY under guard.
ROWE. Bring him forward.
So, my lord. You have ridden through these streets often enough, though never with so many eyes upon you. What answer have you for the farmers whose grain paid your levies? For the families who watched their sons march away to wars that yielded them nothing?
VARNEY. If I am to answer, I ask only to be heard.
ROWE. Heard? The people have listened for years. Now it is your turn.
HUGH. Aye, let him account for the tolls.
MARTIN. And for every new charge that came with them.
ROWE. We were told the kingdom lacked money. Yet the palace grew finer. New monuments rose. New offices appeared. Somehow there was always enough silver for that.
You filled the city with officials, inspectors, secretaries. Men who knew every figure in a ledger and not a single laborer whose life supplied it.
HUGH. True enough.
ROWE. Summonses were sent to poor men who scarcely knew their letters. When they failed to answer in the proper language, they paid fines. When they could not pay, they lost what little they had.
VARNEY. The law cannot govern itself.
COOPER. It governs us well enough.
Laughter among the crowd.
ROWE. Tell me, my lord. When was the last time you worked with your hands?
VARNEY. My office required other duties.
ROWE. Other duties. Your horse wears ornaments worth more than a laborer's yearly earnings.
MARTIN. Some men would be glad merely to own the horse.
VARNEY. You see the trappings and think you know the man beneath them.
ROWE. Then enlighten us.
VARNEY. Gladly.
You speak of tolls. Those tolls repaired bridges after the floods. You speak of levies. The borders did not defend themselves. During the famine in the northern counties, grain was released from my stores. When merchants threatened to abandon the harbor, I persuaded them to remain. Trade continued. The city prospered because of it.
A few in the crowd exchange uncertain looks.
HUGH. He has an answer for everything.
MARTIN. Men like him always do.
VARNEY. Not an answer—facts.
You may condemn me if you wish. But consider what follows if every record is burned and every magistrate driven away. Who settles disputes over land? Who decides inheritance? Who keeps neighbors from settling every quarrel with a club and a knife?
ROWE. You speak as though order and justice were the same thing.
VARNEY. They are not. Yet one seldom survives long without the other.
The crowd grows quieter.
VARNEY. I do not claim perfection. No man in office can. Mistakes were made; some by me. Yet a government is not built in a day, nor repaired by rage alone.
POTTER. There is sense in that.
COOPER. Some sense.
ROWE. Enough.
The crowd falls silent.
You plead your cause skillfully, my lord. Whether skill and innocence are companions is another matter.
VARNEY. Then let the people judge. I ask no privilege beyond a fair hearing.
ROWE. You shall have it. We will hear every charge before sentence is spoken.
HUGH. Come along.
VARNEY. I come willingly. Rumor has had its turn. Let judgment now have its own.
They lead him away. The crowd follows after, arguing among themselves.
Exeunt.

SCENE I. A Physician’s Shop.
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN.
The night grows old, yet sleep comes not to me.
As through the poorer quarter of the town
I pass'd but now, my eye did light upon
A dwelling mean enough to shelter want.
There lives, thought I, a man whom hunger's hand
Hath taught obedience unto desperate needs.
If ever grief should drive a soul to seek
Some dangerous remedy forbidden by law,
That man would furnish it. And now, alas,
The thought returns to serve my present turn.
This should be the place. The shutters are drawn close.
Ho there! Good physician!
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls at such an hour?
ADRIAN.
Come nearer, friend.
I know thy state and will not mock thy poverty.
Here is a purse. Within it lies enough
To ease thy household for a month or more.
Give me a draught that works both swift and sure.
PHYSICIAN.
You ask a fearful thing. The city's law
Condemns the seller and the buyer both.
ADRIAN.
The city's law? What comfort hath it brought thee?
I see want written plainly in thy face.
Your garments plead against your fortune's health.
The law preserves the rich who fashioned it;
It leaves the poor to wrestle with the cold.
PHYSICIAN.
Yet still it is the law.
ADRIAN.
And still thou starv'st.
Take this reward and make an end of doubt.
PHYSICIAN.
My conscience struggles, sir.
ADRIAN.
Let hunger judge.
PHYSICIAN.
Then hear me. I possess a certain powder.
Mix it with wine, and ere an hour has pass'd
The strongest heart shall cease its beating.
ADRIAN.
Give it me.
PHYSICIAN.
Take it, then. Heaven forgive us both.
ADRIAN.
There is thy gold. Strange metal, yellow gold,
How many crimes are hidden in thy shine.
More lives are ruin'd by the thirst of thee
Than by the deadliest mixtures ever brewed.
Farewell. Spend wisely what necessity
Hath wrung from me and purchased from thy hand.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Would that my need had been less great tonight.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar’s Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS with a letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian! Ho!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You are return'd at last. What news is brought?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Ill fortune crossed me. Seeking out a guide
To bear this letter to the northern road,
I came upon a village under watch.
A fever had arisen in the place.
The magistrates, fearing the spread of death,
Forbade all travel through the guarded streets
And held us there three days against our will.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
Then was the message never carried forth?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
It was not. Here it is, seal'd as before.
No messenger would take it from my hand;
Such terror had the sickness bred in them.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is unhappy news. That letter bore
Instructions of great weight and consequence.
Its failure may produce a grievous end.
Go quickly now. Bring me a lantern, friend,
And tools to force a rusted chapel door.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return with speed.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
The hours run fast.
Before the dawn, the maiden hidden there
Will wake and find herself alone and fearful.
Had Adrian known what fortune has delay'd,
His heart might yet have been preserv'd from harm.
I must away at once and trust that time,
Though wounded, may be heal'd before it dies.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE with flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the light. Stand further off, good boy.
Yet keep thy hearing sharp. Beneath that elm
Lie close and watch the pathway to the tomb.
If any traveler should approach this place,
Whistle at once and let me know of it.
PAGE.
My lord, the churchyard chills me.
RENALD.
Fear it not.
Take these commands and follow them.
PAGE retires.
RENALD.
Poor Isabel, untimely laid to rest,
I bring these flowers where happier gifts were due.
The earth receives what should have graced a throne.
Each night I come and water this cold stone
With tears that answer not my grief at all.
Would I had died before I saw this day.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Ha! That is warning. Someone draws near.
I will conceal myself and learn who comes.
He withdraws into the shadows.

SCENE I. A Physician's Shop.
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN.
No rest tonight. My thoughts drive sleep away.
As I came through the poorer streets, I passed a narrow house whose walls seemed ready to fall about their owner. I remember thinking then that misery makes bargains prosperity would never entertain. If a desperate man required some forbidden thing, there lived one who might provide it.
And now I find myself at his door.
This should be the place.
Ho! Is anyone within?
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls?
ADRIAN.
A friend, if money can purchase friendship. Come nearer.
I see enough in your face to know your circumstances. Here—take this purse. In return, I ask for a certain remedy.
PHYSICIAN.
What remedy?
ADRIAN.
One that leaves no time for suffering.
PHYSICIAN.
Sir, speak lower.
Such medicines exist, but the law forbids them. Were I discovered selling one, it would cost me my life.
ADRIAN.
And what sort of life is left to you now?
Your coat is worn thin. Hunger has hollowed your cheeks. The law threatens you with death, yet it has done little to preserve your living.
PHYSICIAN.
You press a hard argument.
ADRIAN.
Need presses harder.
Take the gold.
PHYSICIAN.
I do not like this business.
ADRIAN.
Nor I. Yet here we stand.
PHYSICIAN.
Then listen carefully. I have a powder. Mixed into wine or water, it works swiftly. A healthy man would not survive the hour.
ADRIAN.
That is enough.
PHYSICIAN.
May Heaven forgive me.
ADRIAN.
And me as well.
There is your payment. Strange thing, gold. Men condemn poisons and outlaw them, yet this metal has ruined more souls than any apothecary's vial. It has bought betrayals, murders, wars, and false friendships.
Keep it. Buy food. Mend your household.
Farewell.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Poor comfort. Yet poor comfort is sometimes all that poverty receives.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar's Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS carrying a sealed letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You have returned at last. Was the message delivered?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
No.
I had scarcely begun my journey when I was detained outside the western quarter. A sickness had broken out there. The authorities closed the roads and posted guards at every entrance.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
For how long?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Three days.
No one was permitted to leave. No messenger would accept a letter from our hands. Fear traveled faster than the disease itself.
He gives him the letter.
Here. It remains unopened.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is grave news.
The contents were urgent. Had they been merely courteous words, the delay would matter little. Instead, every hour lost increases the danger.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
What is to be done?
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
We must act without waiting.
Fetch me a lantern. Bring also the iron tools from the storeroom. Quickly.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return directly.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
What cruel fortune chooses its moments so precisely.
Before dawn, the young woman concealed within the chapel will wake. Adrian should already know the truth. Instead he remains ignorant, and ignorance is often more dangerous than malice.
There is no time left for wishing.
I must go myself.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE carrying flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the lantern.
Now go and wait beneath that elm tree. Stay where you can watch the path. If anyone approaches the tomb, whistle to me at once.
PAGE.
My lord, I would rather be anywhere else than in a churchyard at night.
RENALD.
You are not the first to feel so.
Take courage and do as I ask.
PAGE.
I will.
He retires.
RENALD.
Isabel.
I bring flowers again, though they are poor substitutes for the life that should have been yours. This stone receives them gladly enough, but it returns nothing.
Every night I come here. Every night I tell myself it eases the grief.
It does not.
Still I return.
He lays down the flowers.
The earth has claimed too fair a treasure. Had fate shown a little mercy, these blossoms would have adorned a wedding feast rather than a grave.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Someone comes.
Either a mourner or a wanderer. I would know which.
He withdraws into the shadows.
Exeunt.

SCENE V. A blasted moor. Thunder. Enter three ENCHANTRESSES meeting MORWENA.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Mistress, you seem displeased.
MORWENA.
And well I may. What boldness led you forth
To meddle with the fate of Lord Arcturus,
Speaking dark promises into his ear,
Yet never once consulting me, whose hand
Directs the hidden workings of our craft?
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
We meant no slight.
MORWENA.
Yet slight it was.
You toyed with matters touching crowns and blood,
And all for one ambitious mortal man
Who values power above those who assist him.
Such men use wonders as they use their swords:
To serve themselves, then cast them by forgotten.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
What would you have us do?
MORWENA.
Repair the fault.
At dawn assemble by the sunken well
That lies beneath the blackthorn grove. He comes.
His thoughts already race beyond his station.
He longs to know what fortune waits ahead.
Prepare your phials and enchanted roots.
Bring charms collected under moonless skies.
This night I labor toward a greater end.
Upon the western wind there rides a shade,
Half born of dream and half of mortal fear.
Before the morning star ascends the heavens,
I shall compel it to assume a shape
And whisper false assurances to him.
He shall believe himself beyond all danger.
He shall mistake desire for destiny,
And confidence for wisdom. Thus he falls.
For nothing serves destruction more completely
Than a man persuaded he cannot fail.
Music within.
I hear my summons. See, my familiar waits
Among the drifting vapors of the marsh.
Exit.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Come. We have much to gather ere she returns.
Exeunt.
SCENE VI. A chamber in the royal fortress.
Enter VARREN and another NOBLE.
VARREN.
My thoughts and yours, I think, have traveled alike.
The kingdom bears strange burdens these last months.
NOBLE.
It does indeed.
VARREN.
Our gracious sovereign died most suddenly.
Lord Arcturus lamented him with tears;
Yet somehow every road of consequence
Has led directly to Lord Arcturus' gain.
The late commander perished on a hunt.
His son fled shortly after. Many say
Flight proves the crime, though proof is thin enough.
NOBLE.
Few dare examine such conclusions now.
VARREN.
Wisely, perhaps.
The new-made ruler punishes suspicion
Almost as swiftly as rebellion itself.
Those absent from his feasts attract remark.
Those speaking freely earn unwelcome notice.
NOBLE.
Have you heard news of Lord Cedric?
VARREN.
Only rumors.
NOBLE.
Then hear this. He has crossed the sea in secret
To seek the rightful heir.
VARREN.
The prince lives still?
NOBLE.
In safety, so it is reported.
A neighboring king extends protection to him,
Together with such counsel and support
As fortune still allows.
VARREN.
Then hope survives.
NOBLE.
More than survives. Lord Cedric seeks assistance.
If foreign swords and loyal hearts unite,
The realm may yet recover from its wounds.
We hunger now for ordinary blessings:
Peace at our tables, rest throughout the night,
Justice untainted by fear or favor.
VARREN.
And what says Arcturus to these reports?
NOBLE.
He gathers troops. Messengers ride daily.
Something is brewing.
VARREN.
Cedric was summoned home?
NOBLE.
He was.
VARREN.
And answered?
NOBLE.
With a firm refusal. The envoy returned
Bearing little more than cold civility
And warnings left unspoken.
VARREN.
A prudent choice.
NOBLE.
A necessary one.
VARREN.
May some good spirit speed across the sea
And aid the prince before the storm arrives.
This suffering land has endured enough.
NOBLE.
Amen to that. My prayers shall follow him.
Exeunt.
ACT IV
SCENE I. A cavern beneath a ruined tower. In the center, a brazier burning with green flame. Thunder. Enter the three ENCHANTRESSES.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the raven croaked at midnight's hour.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the marsh-hound answered from afar.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
And now the appointed moment draws near.ADRIAN.
No rest tonight. My thoughts drive sleep away.
As I came through the poorer streets, I passed a narrow house whose walls seemed ready to fall about their owner. I remember thinking then that misery makes bargains prosperity would never entertain. If a desperate man required some forbidden thing, there lived one who might provide it.
And now I find myself at his door.
This should be the place.
Ho! Is anyone within?
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls?
ADRIAN.
A friend, if money can purchase friendship. Come nearer.
I see enough in your face to know your circumstances. Here—take this purse. In return, I ask for a certain remedy.
PHYSICIAN.
What remedy?
ADRIAN.
One that leaves no time for suffering.
PHYSICIAN.
Sir, speak lower.
Such medicines exist, but the law forbids them. Were I discovered selling one, it would cost me my life.
ADRIAN.
And what sort of life is left to you now?
Your coat is worn thin. Hunger has hollowed your cheeks. The law threatens you with death, yet it has done little to preserve your living.
PHYSICIAN.
You press a hard argument.
ADRIAN.
Need presses harder.
Take the gold.
PHYSICIAN.
I do not like this business.
ADRIAN.
Nor I. Yet here we stand.
PHYSICIAN.
Then listen carefully. I have a powder. Mixed into wine or water, it works swiftly. A healthy man would not survive the hour.
ADRIAN.
That is enough.
PHYSICIAN.
May Heaven forgive me.
ADRIAN.
And me as well.
There is your payment. Strange thing, gold. Men condemn poisons and outlaw them, yet this metal has ruined more souls than any apothecary's vial. It has bought betrayals, murders, wars, and false friendships.
Keep it. Buy food. Mend your household.
Farewell.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Poor comfort. Yet poor comfort is sometimes all that poverty receives.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar's Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS carrying a sealed letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You have returned at last. Was the message delivered?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
No.
I had scarcely begun my journey when I was detained outside the western quarter. A sickness had broken out there. The authorities closed the roads and posted guards at every entrance.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
For how long?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Three days.
No one was permitted to leave. No messenger would accept a letter from our hands. Fear traveled faster than the disease itself.
He gives him the letter.
Here. It remains unopened.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is grave news.
The contents were urgent. Had they been merely courteous words, the delay would matter little. Instead, every hour lost increases the danger.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
What is to be done?
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
We must act without waiting.
Fetch me a lantern. Bring also the iron tools from the storeroom. Quickly.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return directly.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
What cruel fortune chooses its moments so precisely.
Before dawn, the young woman concealed within the chapel will wake. Adrian should already know the truth. Instead he remains ignorant, and ignorance is often more dangerous than malice.
There is no time left for wishing.
I must go myself.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE carrying flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the lantern.
Now go and wait beneath that elm tree. Stay where you can watch the path. If anyone approaches the tomb, whistle to me at once.
PAGE.
My lord, I would rather be anywhere else than in a churchyard at night.
RENALD.
You are not the first to feel so.
Take courage and do as I ask.
PAGE.
I will.
He retires.
RENALD.
Isabel.
I bring flowers again, though they are poor substitutes for the life that should have been yours. This stone receives them gladly enough, but it returns nothing.
Every night I come here. Every night I tell myself it eases the grief.
It does not.
Still I return.
He lays down the flowers.
The earth has claimed too fair a treasure. Had fate shown a little mercy, these blossoms would have adorned a wedding feast rather than a grave.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Someone comes.
Either a mourner or a wanderer. I would know which.
He withdraws into the shadows.
Exeunt.

SCENE V. A blasted moor. Thunder. Enter three ENCHANTRESSES meeting MORWENA.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Mistress, you are angered.
MORWENA.
Would you have me otherwise?
You have been whispering in Lord Arcturus' ear again, filling his mind with riddles and promises, and never once thought to seek my counsel. Crowns, bloodshed, succession—these are not trifles to be played with.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
We meant only to guide him.
MORWENA.
Guide him? You flatter yourselves.
The man cares nothing for you. He listens because your words feed his ambition. Give him a throne and he would forget every hand that helped him climb to it.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
What would you have done?
MORWENA.
What should have been done.
Tomorrow, before sunrise, meet me at the old well beneath the blackthorn trees. He will come there. His appetite for prophecy grows by the hour. One answer breeds two questions, and every question drives him further on.
Bring what is needed. Roots, oils, charms—whatever remains from the last working.
As for me, I have another task.
There drifts tonight above the western hills a wandering spirit born of fear and longing. Before dawn I shall bind it and give it voice. It will tell Arcturus exactly what he wishes to hear.
That is the surest road to ruin.
Let a man believe himself untouchable and he begins to mistake recklessness for courage. He stops listening. He ceases to doubt. Soon enough he digs the pit into which he falls.
Music within.
There. The summons comes.
My little servant waits among the marsh-fog. I must not keep it long.
Exit.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Come quickly. We have gathering to do.
Exeunt.
SCENE VI. A chamber in the royal fortress.
Enter VARREN and a NOBLE.
VARREN.
The kingdom feels different now.
NOBLE.
It does.
VARREN.
Not long ago men spoke openly. Now they lower their voices before they finish a sentence.
NOBLE.
Fear teaches caution.
VARREN.
A costly lesson.
The king's death still troubles me. Lord Arcturus mourned him publicly and inherited much from the mourning. The commander died soon after. Then the commander's son vanished. Every event seems to point in one direction, though no man dares say where.
NOBLE.
Some things are dangerous even to suspect.
VARREN.
True.
Yet silence has become dangerous too.
NOBLE.
Have you heard from Lord Cedric?
VARREN.
Nothing certain.
NOBLE.
Then I shall tell you. He has crossed the sea and joined the prince.
VARREN.
The prince still lives?
NOBLE.
So all reports agree. He resides under the protection of a neighboring king, who has received him with honor.
VARREN.
That is welcome news.
NOBLE.
Better still, Cedric seeks support for a return. There are noblemen abroad who have not forgotten their loyalties.
VARREN.
Nor, I think, those at home.
NOBLE.
No. Though many keep their thoughts hidden.
A pause.
I think people desire simple things now. Peaceful meals. Undisturbed sleep. The ability to speak honestly without first looking over a shoulder.
VARREN.
Things once taken for granted.
NOBLE.
Exactly.
VARREN.
And Arcturus?
NOBLE.
He prepares. Soldiers are being called in from distant regions. Couriers ride day and night.
VARREN.
Then he knows.
NOBLE.
Or fears.
VARREN.
Was Cedric ordered to return?
NOBLE.
Yes.
VARREN.
And refused?
NOBLE.
Without hesitation. The messenger came back displeased and not a little frightened. Whatever words passed between them, they were enough to darken the man's countenance.
VARREN.
Then events move quickly.
NOBLE.
They do.
VARREN.
May Heaven protect the prince. The land has suffered under too dark a cloud already.
NOBLE.
I shall pray for the same.
Exeunt.
ACT IV
SCENE I. A cavern beneath a ruined tower. A brazier burns in the center. Thunder. Enter the three ENCHANTRESSES.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the raven sounded from the crag.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
And twice the marsh-hound answered through the mist.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
Hush now. The hour approaches.

LORD VAREK.
Away with your remedies. I have no use for them.
Bring me my sword. The breastplate too. Make haste.
Captain, see the watch is doubled on the walls.
PHYSICIAN.
My lord—
VAREK.
No more. The nobles leave me one by one.
If you possessed such skill as men report,
You would not spend it curing fevers only.
Look rather on this realm. There lies the sickness.
Its veins are clogged with treason and with fear.
Find me a draught to cleanse the kingdom whole,
And I shall praise your art above all others.
PHYSICIAN.
The city speaks of armies drawing near.
VAREK.
So much I know.
A thousand rumors blow about the land,
Yet walls are stone, and stone is not persuaded
By every wind that passes overhead.
Bring my armor after me.
Exit VAREK with attendants.
PHYSICIAN.
Were I beyond these gates before the dawn,
No purse could tempt me back.
Exit.
SCENE IV. A plain before the fortress. Hills in the distance.
Enter PRINCE ALDRIC, GENERAL ROWAN, LORD EDRIC, LORD MARSH, and Soldiers.
ALDRIC.
The end approaches. I can almost feel it.
EDRIC.
The men believe the same.
ROWAN.
What ridge lies yonder?
MARSH.
The ridge of Ashen Hill.
ALDRIC.
Then let each soldier cut a branch and bear it.
The enemy watches from the battlements.
Let us disguise our number till we stand
Almost beneath their walls.
SOLDIERS.
It shall be done.
ROWAN.
We hear the tyrant remains within the fortress.
MARSH.
He trusts its strength.
ALDRIC.
Because little else remains to him.
Many who once proclaimed their loyalty
Have vanished at the hour they were most needed.
Those who remain obey from habit or necessity.
Few hearts are truly his.
EDRIC.
The cause will soon be judged by steel, not words.
ROWAN.
Aye. Speculation serves until the battle.
Thereafter deeds pronounce the final sentence.
ALDRIC.
Forward, then.
Exeunt marching.
SCENE V. Within the fortress.
Enter LORD VAREK, CAPTAIN, and Soldiers.
VAREK.
Raise every banner. Let them see our colors.
The cry continues: “They approach, they approach.”
Let them.
These walls have endured storms before.
We shall outlast this one as well.
Had those who owed me service kept their faith,
We might have met the rebels in the field
And scattered them before they reached these gates.
A cry is heard within.
What sound was that?
CAPTAIN.
The women of the household, my lord.
Exit CAPTAIN.
VAREK.
Once such a noise would have disturbed my blood.
There was a time when midnight tales of murder
Could chill me where I stood. No longer.
I have lived too long among calamities.
Disaster has become a familiar guest.
The mind grows accustomed even to horrors.
Re-enter CAPTAIN.
Why do they cry?
CAPTAIN.
Your sister, my lord, is dead.
VAREK.
Dead?
A strange word. It waits for every house,
Yet when it enters one's own door it seems
An unexpected visitor.
There would have been an hour to mourn her properly.
Now time itself feels broken into fragments.
What are our years? A handful of brief moments
Joined together and mistaken for a life.
We labor, scheme, contend, acquire, and lose;
Then vanish. Another takes our place.
The world remembers little.
Enter a SCOUT.
SCOUT.
My lord, I bring a report.
VAREK.
Speak quickly.
SCOUT.
I scarcely trust my own eyes.
VAREK.
Then trust your tongue.
SCOUT.
From the western tower I looked toward Ashen Hill.
At first I thought the forest stirring in the wind.
But soon I saw it move as one body.
VAREK.
What nonsense is this?
SCOUT.
No nonsense, my lord.
Within an hour you may observe it plainly.
The trees advance.
VAREK.
The trees?
SCOUT.
Or men concealed among them.
VAREK.
If this proves false, your reward shall be a rope.
SCOUT.
Then I accept the risk.
VAREK.
Leave me.
Exit SCOUT.
The signs return to trouble me at last.
I trusted too readily in assurances,
Mistaking confidence for certainty.
Yet whether prophecy deceived me or not,
The choice before me is unchanged.
Sound the alarm.
Arm every man who can still bear a weapon.
If fate has come at last, let it find me standing.
Exeunt.

SCENE III. Within the fortress.

Enter LORD VAREK, a PHYSICIAN, attendants.

VAREK.
Enough. Take your medicines elsewhere. I am weary of hearing what cannot be cured.

My sword. Where is it? And the armor.

To an attendant.

See that the watch remains alert. No gate is to be left unwatched tonight.

PHYSICIAN.
My lord, if I might—

VAREK.
You might do many things, if skill were equal to hope.

Men praise physicians for restoring sick bodies. Yet look beyond these walls. There is a nation wasting away. Distrust in every province. Fear in every household.

If you could diagnose that illness and draw it out by the root, I would honor you more than any general.

PHYSICIAN.
The reports from the countryside grow worse.

VAREK.
Reports always grow worse.

Yesterday it was famine. Last week rebellion. Before that, plague. The kingdom survives them all.

Still, bring my armor after me.

Exit VAREK with attendants.

PHYSICIAN.
The kingdom may survive. Whether its ruler does is another matter.

Exit.

SCENE IV. Open country before the fortress.

Enter PRINCE ALDRIC, GENERAL ROWAN, LORD EDRIC, LORD MARSH, and Soldiers.

ALDRIC.
We are close now. Closer than we have ever been.

ROWAN.
The men can feel it.

MARSH.
What woodland lies ahead?

EDRIC.
Ashen Hill Wood.

ALDRIC.
Good.

Let every soldier cut a branch and carry it before him. From the walls they will see a forest where an army stands. Let uncertainty do part of our work for us.

SOLDIERS.
We shall.

ROWAN.
The latest scouts agree on one point: Varek remains inside the fortress.

ALDRIC.
Where else should he be?

He has lost too many allies to trust the open field. Men continue to serve him, but not with their hearts. Some obey from fear, others because they see no safe path away.

MARSH.
A ruler may command hands long after he has lost loyalty.

EDRIC.
Before long the matter leaves the realm of opinion. Battle settles arguments that words only prolong.

ROWAN.
Then let us advance and learn the truth of it.

Exeunt marching.

SCENE V. Within the fortress.

Enter VAREK, CAPTAIN, Soldiers.

VAREK.
Hang every banner.

If they mean to besiege us, let them look upon our colors and know we remain unbroken.

The cry never changes. "They come." Always they come.

Let them.

Stone walls do not frighten easily. Hunger and weather are better allies than panic.

Had certain men remembered their oaths, we would have met this army in the field and driven it back before it reached our borders.

A cry is heard within.

What was that?

CAPTAIN.
The women, my lord.

Exit CAPTAIN.

VAREK.
The women.

There was a time such a sound would have chilled me.

A scream in the dark. A tale of murder. The rumor of some disaster waiting beyond a door. Once my imagination did half the work for fear.

Now it grows tired.

I have watched too many calamities arrive. After a while they cease to surprise.

Re-enter CAPTAIN.

Why do they cry?

CAPTAIN.
Your sister has died, my lord.

A pause.

VAREK.
Has she?

Another pause.

Poor soul.

Yet death has been circling this house so long that I scarcely know how to greet the news. There should be time to mourn. Time to sit with sorrow and give it its due.

But the world rarely waits for proper moments.

A life ends. Another day begins. The wheel turns.

We spend our years pursuing victories that fade almost as soon as they are won. We build, destroy, love, hate, remember, forget.

Then someone else inherits the unfinished business.

Enter a SCOUT in haste.

SCOUT.
My lord.

VAREK.
What now?

SCOUT.
I bring strange news.

VAREK.
Those are seldom welcome words.

SCOUT.
I stood watch on the western tower. Looking toward Ashen Hill, I thought at first that the wind had stirred the forest.

Then I looked again.

VAREK.
And?

SCOUT.
The forest was moving.

VAREK.
Take care.

SCOUT.
I speak what I saw. The trees advance across the plain.

Or rather, men concealed beneath them.

Within the hour they may be visible from the walls.

VAREK.
If this is some frightened mistake—

SCOUT.
It is not.

VAREK.
No.

A silence.

No, perhaps it is not.

There are promises that seem solid until the day they are tested. Warnings too. We hear what pleases us and call it certainty.

He turns away.

Sound the alarm.

Let every able man take up arms. Open the armories. Prepare the gates.

Whether fortune favors us or abandons us, the matter approaches quickly now.

CAPTAIN.
At once, my lord.

VAREK.
Come, then. If the end has chosen this day, I would rather meet it awake than hiding from it.

Exeunt.

LEON.
You have turned my words upside down.
FELIX.
Then they stand upon their heads and see the world better.
LEON.
A strange philosophy.
FELIX.
The only kind worth having.
LEON.
You would make wisdom out of nonsense.
FELIX.
Nay, wisdom is but nonsense that hath prospered.
LEON.
There is a sentence fit for hanging in a tavern.
FELIX.
Then let it hang there. It shall keep better company than in a church.
LEON.
Your wit runs loose today.
FELIX.
Like a hound slipped from the leash.
LEON.
Take care it does not run itself lame.
FELIX.
Fear not. It feeds upon its own speed.
LEON.
A lean diet.
FELIX.
Yet enough to keep it alive.
MARCUS.
Gentlemen, if words were arrows, the town would need rebuilding.
FELIX.
And if silence were gold, you would be the richest man in it.
MARCUS.
A poor fortune indeed.
FELIX.
Poor? Silence costs nothing and is seldom purchased.
LEON.
You praise silence loudly.
FELIX.
Someone must speak for it.
Enter HOUSEKEEPER and SERVANT.
LEON.
See there. A ship enters harbor.
FELIX.
A ship? I see rather the harbor entering the ship.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Thomas!
SERVANT.
Madam?
HOUSEKEEPER.
My basket.
FELIX.
Good Thomas, hide thy face behind it. The basket bears itself more bravely.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Good day, gentlemen.
FELIX.
And a good day returned.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You seem merry.
FELIX.
We labor to keep sadness unemployed.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Then you are diligent workers.
LEON.
Especially he.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Can either of you direct me to young Master Leon?
LEON.
That depends. If you seek him quickly, he is near. If wisely, he may be difficult to find.
HOUSEKEEPER.
A plain answer would serve.
LEON.
Then I am the man.
HOUSEKEEPER.
So I suspected.
FELIX.
A triumph of investigation.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Sir, I would speak privately.
MARCUS.
There is mystery in that request.
FELIX.
Or debt.
LEON.
You think all messages concern debt.
FELIX.
Most do. The remainder concern marriage, which is debt with ceremony.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You are an impertinent fellow.
FELIX.
Madam, I strive for consistency.
LEON.
Come, enough.
FELIX.
Very well. I leave you to your secrets. Marcus, shall we seek dinner?
MARCUS.
Gladly.
FELIX.
Farewell, worthy lady. Farewell, basket. Farewell, Thomas, guardian of baskets.
Exeunt FELIX and MARCUS.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Who is that man who spends words as though they cost him nothing?
LEON.
A friend. He speaks first and reflects afterward.
HOUSEKEEPER.
I noticed as much.
If he had mocked me further, I should have answered him.
SERVANT.
I was ready, madam.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You were ready to do nothing.
SERVANT.
I was ready to begin doing something.
HOUSEKEEPER.
That is what you always say.
SERVANT.
And one day it shall be true.

LEON.
You have twisted my meaning completely.

FELIX.
Then perhaps it was bent to begin with.

LEON.
You never miss a chance.

FELIX.
To improve another man's argument? Rarely.

LEON.
Improve it?

FELIX.
Certainly. I take ordinary nonsense and give it polish.

LEON.
I thought the nonsense was yours.

FELIX.
Only in part. I borrow freely.

MARCUS.
That much is evident.

FELIX.
There speaks a man who contributes little and judges much.

MARCUS.
A comfortable arrangement.

LEON.
Your wit is restless today.

FELIX.
It has had no proper exercise. If I do not let it run about, it becomes destructive.

LEON.
More destructive than usual?

FELIX.
I hope not.

Enter HOUSEKEEPER and SERVANT.

LEON.
Here comes company.

FELIX.
Aye, and with purpose. Casual visitors do not march so directly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Thomas!

SERVANT.
Madam?

HOUSEKEEPER.
My basket. Have you lost it again?

SERVANT.
No, madam.

FELIX.
A remarkable achievement. Let it be recorded.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Good day, gentlemen.

FELIX.
A very good day, if appearances may be trusted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
And if they may not?

FELIX.
Then we are all deceived together.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You have an answer for everything.

FELIX.
Not at all. Only for things I do not understand.

LEON.
Which explains the quantity.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Can someone tell me where I might find Master Leon?

LEON.
You have found him already, though perhaps not improved by the discovery.

HOUSEKEEPER.
That remains to be seen.

FELIX.
A cautious judgment. I approve.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Sir, I would have a private word.

MARCUS.
Now there is a phrase that always awakens curiosity.

FELIX.
Or anxiety.

LEON.
Usually in those not invited to hear it.

FELIX.
A cruel custom.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Will you leave us, then?

FELIX.
Since I am plainly unwanted, I shall preserve what remains of my dignity and go willingly.

MARCUS.
You cannot preserve what was never there.

FELIX.
A painful farewell gift.

Come, Marcus. Let us find food before philosophy becomes necessary.

MARCUS.
Lead on.

FELIX.
Madam, farewell. Thomas, keep watch over the basket. It appears to depend upon you.

Exeunt FELIX and MARCUS.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Who is that fellow?

LEON.
A good friend, though not always at convenient times.

HOUSEKEEPER.
He speaks enough for three men.

LEON.
And listens for half of one.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Had he continued much longer, I should have put him in his place.

SERVANT.
I would have assisted.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You?

SERVANT.
Certainly.

HOUSEKEEPER.
You never assist until the matter is over.

SERVANT.
That is unfortunate timing, not lack of courage.

HOUSEKEEPER.
Keep telling yourself so.

SERVANT.
I do, madam. It is a great comfort.

A herald sounds within.
HERALD. Make way there! The noble challengers approach the field.
Enter a Knight and his Squire.
KNIGHT. Good sir, announce me to the king.
HERALD. Your name and country?
KNIGHT. I am a lord of Macedon, come from distant lands to prove my worth before the princess.
HERALD. Stand forth, then.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, Lords, and Attendants.
LEONTES. What knight is this that seeks our favour?
KNIGHT. My gracious lord, though fortune hath not smiled on me of late, yet courage remains. Hearing of your daughter's beauty and virtue, I have ventured hither to contend among these worthy men.
HELENA. Sir knight, your courtesy becomes you well. What device bear you on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon mounting through the clouds toward a star; beneath is written, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. A noble emblem. It speaks of one whose hopes rise higher than his condition.
KNIGHT. My lord, a man may be poor in treasure and yet rich in honour. If I prevail, let merit be my witness; if I fail, I shall still esteem the journey well spent.
LEONTES. You answer wisely. Many boast before the contest begins; few speak so modestly.
FIRST LORD. The other challengers are assembled and await your command.
LEONTES. Then let the trumpets sound. This day shall prove what metal lies beneath bright armour.
Trumpets flourish.
HELENA. Father, I confess these spectacles delight me less for victory than for the virtues they reveal. True worth is not always known until it is tried.
LEONTES. Well said, daughter. The furnace discovers the gold, and trial the man.
HERALD. Let the lists be opened!
The Knights prepare themselves.
KNIGHT. Whatever fortune grants, I thank the heavens for this occasion. A brave attempt is itself a kind of triumph.
LEONTES. Go then, and may honour attend the deserving.
Exeunt toward the tournament.

A herald is heard offstage.
HERALD. Room there! The challengers come to the field.
A Knight enters with his Squire.
KNIGHT. Friend, let the king know I am arrived.
HERALD. State your name and country.
KNIGHT. A nobleman of Macedon. I have crossed many seas to seek honour in this court and to offer my service before the princess.
HERALD. Wait here and be presented.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, with Lords and attendants.
LEONTES. Who comes before us?
KNIGHT. My liege, fortune has dealt unevenly with me, yet she has not robbed me of courage. News of your daughter's grace drew me here, and I ask leave to compete among the other knights.
HELENA. You speak with good manners, sir. Tell us, what sign is carried on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon rising through the clouds toward a single star. The motto reads, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. An ambitious image. It suggests a man who aims beyond the limits of his station.
KNIGHT. Perhaps so, my lord. Wealth is uncertain, but honour belongs to any man willing to earn it. Should I succeed, let my deeds speak; should I fail, I shall regret nothing.
LEONTES. A measured answer. Confidence sits better when it is not dressed as pride.
FIRST LORD. Sire, the remaining competitors are ready.
LEONTES. Then there is no cause to delay. Let the trial begin.
Trumpets sound.
HELENA. I often think these contests reveal more than strength. A person's nature is seen most clearly when something important is at stake.
LEONTES. There is wisdom in that. Gold is tested by fire, and character by challenge.
HERALD. Open the lists!
The knights move toward the field.
KNIGHT. Whatever happens today, I count myself fortunate to have stood among such company. Even the attempt carries its own reward.
LEONTES. Go forward, then. May honour find its rightful owner.
They exit toward the tournament grounds.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Is our sovereign yet awake, good sir?
EDMUND. Not so, my lord. He charged me to attend him at first light, yet I fear the hour runs fast.
AURELIAN. I shall conduct you to his chamber.
EDMUND. The duty is a welcome burden, though a burden still.
AURELIAN. Service performed with love sits lightly on the shoulders. Here is the door.
EDMUND. Then with your leave I’ll call upon him.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Will the king depart this morning?
AURELIAN. So he intended when last we spoke.
RICHARD. The heavens were troubled through the night. Fierce winds battered the towers, and many swore they heard strange cries upon the air. Dogs howled without cease, and the ravens gathered before dawn as though they scented calamity.
AURELIAN. The storm was harsh indeed.
RICHARD. In all my years I do not recall its equal.
Re-enter Edmund.
EDMUND. O miserable sight! O dreadful, dreadful day!
AURELIAN, RICHARD. What has befallen?
EDMUND. Ruin has entered where honour slept. A most foul crime has struck at majesty itself and robbed this kingdom of its living heart.
AURELIAN. Explain yourself!
RICHARD. Speak plainly, man.
EDMUND. Go to the chamber and behold it with your own eyes. My tongue refuses what my eyes have witnessed.
Aurelian and Richard exit.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason! Murder! Let every loyal soul arise! Prince Henry! Prince Edward! Wake from sleep and look upon the horror that has visited this house!
A bell rings.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What summons breaks the silence of the dawn? Why does this fearful noise disturb the household?
EDMUND. Madam, forgive me. The news is too terrible for gentle ears.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What means this uproar?
EDMUND. Our gracious king is slain!
ELEANOR. Heaven defend us! Slain?
STANLEY. No, say not so. Let those words be false.
Re-enter Aurelian and Richard with Lords.
AURELIAN. Had death taken me before this hour, I should have counted myself fortunate. From this moment all joy seems hollow. Honour lies dead beside our sovereign.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What disaster has occurred?
AURELIAN. The source from which your lives were nourished is cut off. Your father is no more.
EDMUND. The king has been murdered.
HENRY. By whose hand?
RICHARD. His attendants appeared guilty. Their garments were stained with blood, and the weapons were found beside them.
AURELIAN. In grief and rage I struck them down before they could answer.
EDMUND. Why did you act so quickly?
AURELIAN. Who could remain calm before such a sight? There lay our noble king, his wounds crying out for vengeance. Reason yielded to loyalty and sorrow.
ELEANOR. I grow faint.
STANLEY. Attend the queen.
HENRY. Why do we stand silent while danger gathers around us?
EDWARD. We know not where the blow was forged. It may be aimed at us next.
STANLEY. Let us withdraw for a time, compose ourselves, and then seek the truth. Fear clouds every judgment.
HENRY. Agreed. We must learn who profits from this evil deed.
STANLEY. Until then, I place my trust in Heaven and stand ready against whatever treachery remains concealed.
Exeunt.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Has the king stirred yet?
EDMUND. Not that I know of, my lord. He asked me to attend him early, and I fear I am close to missing the appointed hour.
AURELIAN. Come then. I’ll take you to his chamber.
EDMUND. A pleasant duty, though duties seldom come without trouble.
AURELIAN. Trouble weighs little when the heart is willing. The door is here.
EDMUND. I’ll announce myself.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Does His Majesty leave today?
AURELIAN. That was his intention.
RICHARD. The night gave ill signs of it. The wind shook the walls, and more than one man claimed to hear cries carried through the darkness. Even the dogs would not rest. Before sunrise the ravens circled the courtyard in great numbers.
AURELIAN. A bitter night, certainly.
RICHARD. Stranger than any I remember.
Edmund returns suddenly.
EDMUND. Horror! O God, what horror!
AURELIAN. What has happened?
RICHARD. Speak!
EDMUND. Wickedness has done its worst. The king is struck down. The crown itself has been assaulted.
AURELIAN. What are you saying?
EDMUND. Look for yourselves. I cannot tell it properly.
Aurelian and Richard hurry away.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason and murder! Princes, rise! Throw off your sleep and see what dreadful thing has been done!
A bell sounds throughout the castle.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What alarm is this? Why is the house in such confusion?
EDMUND. Madam, the tale is too dreadful to repeat.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What misfortune stirs everyone so?
EDMUND. Our king has been murdered.
ELEANOR. Murdered?
STANLEY. No. Let that not be true.
Aurelian and Richard return with several lords.
AURELIAN. Had my life ended an hour ago, I should have died happier than I stand now. Everything seems diminished after this.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What has occurred?
AURELIAN. The man who gave you life is gone.
EDMUND. Your father has been slain.
HENRY. By whom?
RICHARD. Suspicion falls upon those who guarded his chamber. They were covered in blood, and bloody daggers lay beside them.
AURELIAN. I killed them in my fury.
EDMUND. Before they were questioned?
AURELIAN. Yes. I saw the king's wounds and lost command of myself. Grief moved faster than judgment.
ELEANOR. I cannot bear this.
STANLEY. Help the queen.
HENRY. We speak too little when the matter concerns us most.
EDWARD. And perhaps too openly. If this plot reaches the throne, who can say where it ends?
STANLEY. Let us gather ourselves before we act. Fear makes poor counsel.
HENRY. Yet the truth must be found.
STANLEY. It shall be. Until then, I trust in Heaven and keep watch against whatever treason still hides in shadow.
Exeunt.

The second Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Princess Helena.
KING LEONTES. Who is this second champion?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, honoured father;
Upon his shield appears a soaring eagle
That climbs above the storm and seeks the sun;
The motto, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A noble thought. True courage oft aspires
Beyond the reach of fortune's harsh command.
The third Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield.
LEONTES. And who comes next?
HELENA. A lord of Venice.
His device is a laurel growing from a rock;
The word, Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. A fitting emblem for a steadfast man.
The fourth Knight passes.
HELENA. The fourth bears a broken sword
Bound fast with golden cords;
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Loyalty can mend what force destroys.
The fifth Knight passes.
HELENA. Upon the fifth knight's shield
A ship rides safely through a violent sea;
Beneath is written, Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope is a pilot many men have trusted.
The sixth Knight enters alone, his armour plain and weathered. He presents his shield himself.
LEONTES. And what says this last device,
Which the knight delivers with such modest grace?
HELENA. A single flower rising through the snow;
The motto, Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. A simple lesson, yet a pleasing one.
Though winter presses hard upon his state,
He trusts that spring may yet return.
FIRST LORD. His appearance promises but little.
His armour seems more acquainted with neglect
Than with the honours of the field.
SECOND LORD. Yet strangers often wear disguises
That hide their worth.
THIRD LORD. Or perhaps he kept that rust
To polish it with glory in the lists.
LEONTES. Men judge too swiftly by what they can see.
The garment does not always reveal the man.
But look—the contest ends. Let us retire.
Exeunt. Great shouts within. Voices cry, “The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!”

SCENE III. A Banquet Hall
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. My lords and noble knights,
To welcome you would waste unnecessary words.
Your deeds have spoken loudly for themselves.
This hall is prepared for joy, and joy becomes
Such company as yours.
Take now your places.
HELENA. And you, good sir,
To whom this garland of victory belongs,
Receive it as the honour of the day.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, fortune has been kinder than my merits.
LEONTES. Call it fortune if you wish;
The prize remains deserved.
Not all men are fashioned in equal measure.
Some achieve honour, while others seem born
To surpass it.
Come, daughter, lead the feast.
KNIGHTS. We thank Your Majesty.
LEONTES. You do us honour by your presence.
MARSHAL. Sir, your seat awaits.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Another may deserve it more.
FIRST KNIGHT. Not so.
We who have lost bear no resentment,
Nor should excellence be denied its due.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Your generosity exceeds my deserts.
LEONTES. Sit, sir, sit.
Aside.
By heaven, I marvel at my daughter.
The feast stands before her untouched,
Yet all her thoughts seem fixed elsewhere.
HELENA. Aside.
The richest dishes lose their savour.
My eyes have feasted where my heart would dine.
Surely this knight possesses uncommon virtue.
LEONTES. Aside.
He is but a gentleman,
No different from the rest.
A broken lance or two has earned him praise.
Let that suffice.
HELENA. Aside.
To compare the others with him
Is to compare crystal with a star.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
That king recalls my father's image,
Majestic in the days of his prosperity.
Around him gathered nobles like bright planets
Circling a sun.
Now I remain, diminished by misfortune,
A lonely spark where once a fire burned.
Thus time exalts and humbles whom it pleases;
It grants its gifts, then asks them back again.

The second Knight passes, and his Squire presents a shield before Helena.
LEONTES. Who offers himself next?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, my lord.
His shield bears an eagle climbing through dark clouds toward the sun.
The motto reads, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A bold sentiment. Some men would rather challenge fate than submit to it.
A third Knight approaches.
LEONTES. And this one?
HELENA. A Venetian nobleman.
Upon his shield grows a laurel from bare stone.
His word is Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. Glory won through hardship. An old lesson, but a worthy one.
The fourth Knight presents his device.
HELENA. A shattered sword bound together with golden cords.
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Faith and loyalty often repair what strength cannot.
The fifth Knight advances.
HELENA. He bears a vessel crossing rough seas.
Below it stands the phrase Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope has carried many safely through greater storms.
The last Knight enters without attendant or display. His armour is worn and dull with age.
LEONTES. And what of this final challenger?
HELENA. His shield shows a flower breaking through snow.
The words are Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. Plain enough, yet full of meaning. A man brought low still believes better days may come.
FIRST LORD. His appearance gives little cause for confidence.
SECOND LORD. Appearances have deceived wiser men than us.
THIRD LORD. Perhaps the rust is intentional. Victory might shine brighter beside it.
LEONTES. We are all too eager to read character from clothing. Let us wait for proof instead.
Shouts erupt offstage.
VOICES. The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!
LEONTES. It seems the field has already given its answer.
Exeunt.

SCENE III. A Hall Prepared for Feasting
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. Noble knights, welcome.
Yet your actions have welcomed you better than any words I could offer.
The day calls for celebration, not ceremony.
Take your places and enjoy what has been prepared.
HELENA. And you, sir,
Accept this wreath.
The honour belongs to you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, I fear fortune deserves more credit than I do.
LEONTES. Fortune may help, but she rarely wins tournaments alone.
Today is yours.
HELENA places the wreath upon him.
LEONTES. Daughter, sit beside me and guide the rest to their seats.
KNIGHTS. Your kindness honours us.
LEONTES. The honour is shared.
MARSHAL. Sir, this place has been reserved for you.
STRANGER KNIGHT. There are worthier men present.
FIRST KNIGHT. We disagree.
No gentleman should envy another's success, especially when it has been fairly earned.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Then I thank you for your courtesy.
He sits.
LEONTES. Aside.
My daughter hardly touches her meal.
The victor occupies more of her attention than the feast.
HELENA. Aside.
Nothing on the table pleases me.
My thoughts remain fixed elsewhere.
There is something rare about this man.
LEONTES. Aside.
A capable knight, certainly, but no miracle.
Young hearts are quick to exaggerate.
HELENA. Aside.
The difference seems greater to me.
The others shine, yet he draws the eye without trying.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
The king reminds me of my father as he once stood:
powerful, admired, surrounded by loyal nobles.
Time has altered everything.
Where he blazed like the sun, I wander as a small light in darkness.
So it goes with men. Time raises them high, then lowers them again, caring little for what they desire.
The feast continues.

BALTHAZAR. He hears us not, nor answers to our call. Love hath transformed him into some strange creature.
LUCIUS. Let him alone. Such men are better companions to their thoughts than to their friends.
BALTHAZAR. Yet I shall summon him once more. I conjure thee by Celia's golden hair, by her bright smile and gentle voice, by every grace that nature placed within her, appear before us and confess thy bondage.
LUCIUS. If he hears that, he'll curse thee for it.
BALTHAZAR. Not so. A lover is never angered by praise of his beloved. He would sooner quarrel with the wind than with the sound of her name.
LUCIUS. Look there. He has hidden himself beside the garden wall. The night suits his humour.
BALTHAZAR. Love delights in shadows. Yet if love cannot see, how comes it always to find trouble?
LUCIUS. Come, let us leave him. He seeks solitude, and we waste our breath.
BALTHAZAR. Agreed. Good night to our wandering friend.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. A Garden by Moonlight
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN. They laugh at longing who have never suffered it.
A window opens above. CELIA appears.
What radiance breaks upon the darkness there?
The moon grows pale beside her.
She stands above the garden like a star
That has forgotten heaven and wandered here.
If beauty had a voice, it would be hers.
Yet she speaks not.
Her silence itself is music.
How softly she rests her hand upon her cheek.
Would that I were the breeze that touched her face.
CELIA. Alas.
ADRIAN. She speaks. Let every other sound be still.
CELIA. Why must affection choose such difficult paths?
Why should the one I love belong to those
Whom my family calls enemies?
Were he named differently,
Would he be less kind,
Less worthy,
Less himself?
A name is but a word.
The flower blooming in the garden
Would smell as sweet if called by any other title.
So too the man I love.
What power lies in a name
That it should divide two hearts?
ADRIAN. Aside.
Shall I remain silent, or reveal myself?
CELIA. If he would cast aside that hated name,
I would surrender all hesitation.
ADRIAN. Then hear me.
Call me what you will,
And I shall answer.
The name I bear matters little
If it displeases you.
CELIA. Who speaks from the darkness?
ADRIAN. One who fears his own name
Only because it troubles you.
Were it written before me,
I would gladly blot it out.
CELIA. Though I have heard but little,
The voice is known to me.
You are Adrian.
ADRIAN. And happier now than ever that I am.

BALTHAZAR. He neither hears nor stirs. Love has carried him so far from ordinary company that I doubt he remembers we exist.
LUCIUS. Then leave him to his dreaming.
BALTHAZAR. One call more. By Celia's bright eyes, by the sweetness of her smile, by every charm she possesses, I summon him forth.
LUCIUS. You'll earn his displeasure if he catches you.
BALTHAZAR. Hardly. Lovers forgive much when their lady's name is involved.
LUCIUS. There, among the vines. He's hidden himself away again.
BALTHAZAR. The night is a faithful friend to lovers.
LUCIUS. And an unfaithful one to common sense.
BALTHAZAR. Come. We shall find warmer beds than this garden path.
They leave.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. The Garden
Enter ADRIAN alone.
ADRIAN. Easy enough to mock a wound you've never felt.
A light appears at an upper window. CELIA steps forward.
What light is that?
The whole garden seems altered by it.
The moon, which a moment ago ruled the sky, now looks pale and unnecessary.
There she stands.
If those stars above exchanged places with her eyes, I think the heavens themselves would shine no brighter.
Even the birds might mistake this night for morning.
She leans upon her hand.
To be that hand's companion for a moment—what fortune that would be.
CELIA. Ah me.
ADRIAN. At last, her voice.
CELIA. Why should the person dearest to me carry a name that stands against my own house?
If he were called something else, would anything important change?
Would his heart be different?
His nature?
Names seem powerful, yet they are only sounds.
A rose would remain a rose regardless of what people chose to call it.
Its fragrance would not fade.
Neither would his worth.
ADRIAN. Aside.
Do I wait, or answer her?
CELIA. If he would abandon that single obstacle, I could give him everything.
ADRIAN. Then take me at my word.
Give me another name, and I shall wear it gladly.
CELIA. Who is there?
ADRIAN. A man who has grown tired of hearing his own name spoken as your enemy.
If I could erase it with a stroke, I would.
CELIA. I have not heard many words, yet I know that voice.
It is Adrian.
ADRIAN. Then I am discovered—and fortunate to be so.
They gaze at one another as the night grows quiet.

PISTOL.
Curse thee and all thy counsel! Take thy friendship and choke on it!
FLUELLEN.
That is spoken. It shall be remembered.
PISTOL.
A Spanish fig for thee and thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
Very well. He is gone in anger.
GOWER.
That fellow is no soldier, but a wanderer in borrowed armor. I know him now. A thief of words and a thief of coin both.
FLUELLEN.
He did speak stoutly enough upon the rampart, I will maintain that much. Yet words and deeds are not one thing, no.
GOWER.
He is one of those idle companions who haunt the wars only to adorn themselves with other men’s honor. They learn the names of captains as children learn songs, and later sing them at taverns as if they were present at the deeds.
FLUELLEN.
If I find him again, I shall examine his courage more closely. There may be cracks in his show of valor.
[Drums heard.]
GOWER.
The King approaches.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and Soldiers.
FLUELLEN.
God preserve your Majesty! The passage of the bridge is most nobly kept.
KING HENRY.
Speak, Fluellen. How stands the field?
FLUELLEN.
The enemy has withdrawn, your Majesty, with some disorder. Our men held firm. The Duke of Exeter behaved with great resolution, as any captain might desire.
KING HENRY.
What loss have we sustained?
FLUELLEN.
Very little in truth. A few wounded, none of principal account.
Though there is one matter, if it please your Majesty.
KING HENRY.
Proceed.
FLUELLEN.
A certain soldier, one Bardolph, was taken in theft against a church, and by the law of war he hath been executed.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
He was a man of loud countenance, his face much inflamed, as if fire were lodged within it. Yet such outward appearance is no defense before justice.
KING HENRY.
Let it be known throughout the army: no plundering, no violence against the people of France. What is taken shall be paid for. Discipline shall be our strength, not disorder.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[Tucket sounded.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I come from my sovereign with message.
KING HENRY.
Speak freely. I know thy office.
MONTJOY.
Thus says the King of France: though fortune has not favored us in the field, the matter is not yet concluded. He bids you consider the losses you have sustained, and the long labor of your march, and to weigh whether further resistance serves your advantage.
He offers terms, though not generous ones, for the return of your forces.
KING HENRY.
Is this all?
MONTJOY.
There is more. He reminds you that your position grows weaker with time, and that endurance is not victory. He urges you to accept composition before greater ruin follows.
KING HENRY.
And what of his own condition?
MONTJOY.
He stands prepared for war, yet would prefer resolution by treaty.
KING HENRY.
Then return to him and say this: we are not yet diminished to the point he imagines. Our strength is measured not only in numbers, but in will.
Tell him England has not yet spent her breath. And though we bleed, we do not bend for threats.
As for his terms, we shall consider them in our own time, not his.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry your answer.
KING HENRY.
Go then.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty speaks firmly.
KING HENRY.
Necessity teaches the tongue its shape.
Come, let us see what the next hour brings. War does not pause for negotiation, nor does honor wait upon convenience.
[Exeunt.]

PISTOL.
Rot thee and all thy counsel! Keep thy friendship for fools.
FLUELLEN.
So be it. I have heard thee.
PISTOL.
A fig of Spain for thee and all thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
He is gone off in heat.
GOWER.
He is no soldier. I have seen such men before—vagabonds that creep into wars to clothe themselves in borrowed honor. I know him now well enough: a thief of reputation more than of coin.
FLUELLEN.
He spoke bravely enough upon the bridge, I will say that. Yet a brave tongue is not always a brave heart.
GOWER.
No. These fellows learn the names of captains as one learns a song, then sing it in alehouses afterward, as though they had stood where steel was crossing steel.
FLUELLEN.
If he returns my way, I shall look more closely at his courage. Words are easy things to wear.
[Drums within.]
GOWER.
The King comes on.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge, is it?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and SOLDIERS.
FLUELLEN.
God save your Majesty. The bridge holds well.
KING HENRY.
Tell me then, Fluellen, how goes the field?
FLUELLEN.
In truth, sir, the enemy has drawn back somewhat disorderly. Our men stood firm. Exeter bore himself like a man born to command.
KING HENRY.
Losses?
FLUELLEN.
Not many. A few wounded, nothing that touches the chief strength of your army.
There is one matter I must report, though.
KING HENRY.
Speak it.
FLUELLEN.
A soldier named Bardolph was taken in theft against a church. He has been executed according to the law of war.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
A man with a face all inflamed and broken-looking, as though fire had once lived in him and burned itself out.
KING HENRY.
Let it be proclaimed through the camp: no plunder, no violence against the townsfolk. All must be paid for. Discipline first, always.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[A tucket sounds.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I bring a message from the French King.
KING HENRY.
We know thee. Speak.
MONTJOY.
He says this: though fortune has gone against him in the field, the war is not yet settled. He bids you weigh your losses and consider whether further resistance is worth the cost.
He offers terms for your withdrawal and the return of your men.
KING HENRY.
And that is all?
MONTJOY.
He warns that your strength will diminish if you continue, and urges you to end matters before greater harm follows.
KING HENRY.
What does he think remains of us?
MONTJOY.
He believes you weakened, though still dangerous.
KING HENRY.
Then return and tell him he miscounts us. We are fewer perhaps, but not less resolved.
England has not yet spent itself, and what remains is not for bargaining under pressure.
Say to him also: we will answer his proposals in our own time.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry it.
KING HENRY.
Go.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty holds firm.
KING HENRY.
There is little choice in it. One either stands, or yields.
Come. The day is not finished yet, and neither is the work before us.
[Exeunt.]

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you look.
FOOL.
And thou look’st wiser than thou speak’st, which is a harder deceit to maintain.
SERVANT.
You answer quickly for one held a fool.
FOOL.
Speed is the last refuge of those who have no estate in argument.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Keep near him. I would speak with you apart.
FOOL.
Apart is the safest company.
SERVANT.
I had rather stand aside than be found standing wrong.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I marvel at it still. Why was my estate not set before me in full,
that I might measure my spending against my means?
I was made to believe the ground beneath me firmer than it proved.
MARCUS.
My lord, I spoke at several times, and in as plain a manner as I could devise.
AURELIUS.
You spoke, yes. Yet never at the right hour, or so it seemed to me then.
Some occasions found me unwilling, others found me distracted, and your counsel was set aside as if it were no more than noise at a feast.
MARCUS.
I brought accounts before you. I laid them open without disguise.
You would sometimes cast them back and say they were written to frighten you rather than to inform you.
When gifts were asked for, I hesitated, and you thought it refusal out of neglect. Yet I often begged you to hold your hand, to consider the drain upon your coffers.
AURELIUS.
And now you speak of coffers as if they still held breath.
MARCUS.
They do not, my lord. They are emptied. Some lands are already pledged, others lost outright. What remains can scarcely answer the debts that press upon the present hour.
AURELIUS.
Then let the remainder go also. I would not be haunted by fragments.
MARCUS.
It is already nearly nothing to sell.
AURELIUS.
Nothing? I was told I possessed abundance.
MARCUS.
Abundance that moved too freely is soon called memory rather than possession.
AURELIUS.
So I am poor without knowing the passage into poverty.
MARCUS.
Poverty is not a sudden arrival. It advances while a man is looking elsewhere.
AURELIUS.
And what would you have me do now? Defend the gap between what I am and what I thought myself to be?
MARCUS.
I would have you pause. Consider those to whom you owe. Consider also those who depend upon your house.
AURELIUS.
Pause. That word comes always too late.
MARCUS.
Better late than never spoken.
AURELIUS.
Yet spoken is not the same as answered.
MARCUS.
True.
Enter a MESSENGER.
MESSENGER.
My lord, the creditors press again. They demand settlement without delay.
AURELIUS.
Let them press. There is little left to yield.
MESSENGER.
They threaten suit.
AURELIUS.
Suit may be made of cloth or complaint; I possess neither in plenty now.
MESSENGER.
They ask for what is owed.
AURELIUS.
And I ask for what is possible.
[Exit MESSENGER.]
MARCUS.
The hour grows sharper.
AURELIUS.
Then let it sharpen me. I have been soft too long in my own judgement.
If I trusted too easily, it was not without reason. Men spoke fairly. Promises stood like pillars in my hearing. It is only now, when the roof leans, that I begin to question the strength of those supports.
MARCUS.
The roof has not fallen yet.
AURELIUS.
Not yet. But I hear it complain.
MARCUS.
There is still time to rebuild if you will allow it.
AURELIUS.
Rebuild from what? From absence?
MARCUS.
From what remains.
AURELIUS.
Very little remains.
MARCUS.
Enough to begin.
AURELIUS.
Then begin carefully. I would not lose even the act of beginning.
[Exeunt.]

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you seem.
FOOL.
And you seem wiser than you are. I call that an uneven trade.
SERVANT.
For a fool, you move quickly in reply.
FOOL.
Slowness is a luxury for those who can afford better arguments.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Stay near him a while. I would have speech with you when I can.
FOOL.
I prefer standing near nothing at all. Less chance of error.
SERVANT.
And I prefer stepping aside before I am asked to stand in judgment.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I cannot put my mind to rest on this matter.
Why was my estate never set plainly before me? I might have governed my spending differently had I known the truth in time.
Instead I was led to believe I had more beneath me than I truly did.
MARCUS.
