ELIZABETH. You are determined to laugh at me.
LADY MARGARET. Only because the situation insists upon it. Countess today; who knows what tomorrow may bring?
ELIZABETH. Please, enough. Such talk troubles me more than it pleases me.
LADY MARGARET. Then you possess a rare quality at court.
ELIZABETH. The Queen has troubles enough. I would not willingly become another source of pain for her. Promise me you will not repeat what was said.
LADY MARGARET. My discretion is occasionally better than my reputation suggests.
They depart.

SCENE IV. A Hall of Judgment
Trumpets announce the entrance of officials, clergy, and nobles. The KING takes his seat beneath a canopy, while the QUEEN sits at some distance.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Let order be kept while these proceedings begin.
KING. We need not rehearse what has already been heard. The commission is known to everyone present.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Very well.
CLERK. Summon those required before the court.
CRIER. Let all concerned attend and answer.
The hall falls silent.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. The court is assembled.
KING. Then let us not waste time.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. The record shall note that this hearing proceeds under lawful authority and with the consent of all required parties.
QUEEN. Authority is valuable. Justice is more valuable still.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Both shall be observed, Your Grace.
KING. That is my desire as well. Let no one assume the matter settled before it is heard.
A few lords exchange glances.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Then we may begin.
The hearing proceeds as the court watches in expectation.

“It is a relief to move about after sitting still for so long,” said Miss Harrington.
Charlotte agreed readily, though she suspected there was more civility than necessity in the proposal. The true purpose, however, was not entirely unsuccessful. Mr. Ashford, who had been absorbed in a volume near the fire, raised his eyes from the page. The movement in his direction had not escaped him. After a moment he closed the book and listened.
“You must join us,” said Miss Harrington.
“I fear I cannot,” he replied. “Your walk appears to be founded on one of two intentions, and my presence would spoil either.”
The remark instantly awakened curiosity.
“What intentions?” cried Miss Harrington. “You must explain yourself.”
Charlotte smiled.
“Do not encourage him. He wishes to provoke us, and the surest punishment is to let him keep his secret.”
But Miss Harrington was not so easily discouraged.
“No, indeed. I insist upon an explanation.”
“With pleasure,” said Mr. Ashford. “Either you have chosen to pace the room because you have confidential matters to discuss, in which case I should be an intrusion; or you imagine that walking displays you to greater advantage than sitting, in which case I can admire the exhibition much better from where I am.”
“How intolerable!” exclaimed Miss Harrington. “What shall be done with him?”
“There are many forms of revenge,” said Charlotte. “Ridicule is among the easiest. Surely you know enough of his character to laugh at him effectively.”
“I assure you I do not,” replied Miss Harrington. “One cannot easily laugh at a person who refuses to provide material. Mr. Ashford is entirely too composed.”
“Then he enjoys a rare privilege,” said Charlotte. “Few people are safe from ridicule. I confess I value a laugh too highly not to employ it whenever I can.”
“You give ridicule too honourable a place,” said Mr. Ashford. “The wisest actions may be made absurd by someone determined to see nothing else.”
“True,” said Charlotte, “but I hope I am not one of those people. I do not laugh at wisdom. Folly, however, is another matter. Contradictions, vanities, little absurdities—those are fair game. And I imagine you possess none of them.”
“No person is entirely free from weakness.”
“Vanity, perhaps?”
“Certainly vanity.”
“And pride?”
“Pride, if governed by reason, is less dangerous than vanity.”
Charlotte turned aside to conceal a smile.
“Your investigation seems complete,” said Miss Harrington. “What judgment have you reached?”
“That Mr. Ashford considers himself remarkably free from defects.”
“Not at all,” said he. “I know several of mine. My temper, for example, is not especially forgiving. I do not easily forget injuries, nor do I readily alter an opinion once formed. Some would call me stubborn.”
“That is indeed a fault,” Charlotte answered. “An unwillingness to forgive is never attractive. Still, it is not a fault I find amusing, so you remain secure.”
“I suspect everyone has some natural weakness.”
“And yours is a tendency to think poorly of people.”
“And yours,” he replied with a faint smile, “is a determination to misinterpret them.”
Before Charlotte could answer, Miss Harrington rose abruptly.
“Someone must play the pianoforte,” she declared. “I am exhausted by conversations in which I contribute so little.”

“It feels impossible to sit still any longer,” said Miss Harrington. “Come, let us walk about the room.”
Charlotte joined her at once. The invitation had another purpose besides exercise, and that purpose soon succeeded. Mr. Ashford looked up from the book he had been reading. A moment later, he shut it altogether.
“You ought to join us,” Miss Harrington said.
“I think not.”
“And why?”
“Because your walk is prompted by one of two reasons, and I should interfere with both.”
Naturally, this produced immediate curiosity.
Charlotte laughed. “Pay no attention. He means to puzzle us.”
“That is not enough,” said Miss Harrington. “I demand an explanation.”
Mr. Ashford obliged.
“You are either exchanging secrets, which would make me an inconvenience, or you believe the room offers an excellent stage upon which to display yourselves. In that case, I prefer my current seat as a viewing position.”
Miss Harrington stared at him.
“Really, Mr. Ashford, you are impossible.”
“There is a simple remedy,” Charlotte said. “Laugh at him.”
“If only it were so easy. He never supplies the necessary material.”
“Then he possesses an enviable advantage,” Charlotte replied. “Most people are generous providers of nonsense. I enjoy discovering it.”
Mr. Ashford smiled slightly.
“That habit has ruined many fair judgments. A determined wit can find something ridiculous in almost anything.”
“Almost anything,” Charlotte agreed. “But not everything. I hope I know the difference between genuine merit and mere absurdity. It is the latter that entertains me.”
“And you suppose I have none?”
“I suspect you try very hard not to.”
“No one succeeds entirely.”
“Not even with vanity?”
“Certainly not.”
“Or pride?”
“At least pride may be controlled.”
That answer nearly made Charlotte laugh outright.
Miss Harrington, who had followed the exchange with growing impatience, interrupted.
“Well? What have you discovered about him?”
Charlotte considered.
“He appears to regard himself as nearly faultless.”
“I said nothing of the kind,” said Mr. Ashford. “I am perfectly aware of my defects. My opinions are slow to change. Injuries are slower still to leave my memory. Once I think poorly of someone, I rarely think well again.”
“A serious flaw,” Charlotte replied. “Though not a humorous one. I cannot mock you for it.”
“Every character has its weakness.”
“Then yours is distrusting people.”
He met her eyes.
“And yours is misunderstanding them on purpose.”
For a second neither spoke.
Miss Harrington broke the silence with visible relief.
“Enough philosophy. Let us have music. It is far more agreeable than being ignored in one's own drawing room.”

CAPTAIN. Speak boldly to their marshal. Tell him this:
We are no soldiers dressed for festival,
But men acquainted with the dust of roads.
Our cloaks are stained with weather, mud, and toil;
The bright devices that adorned our ranks
Have long since faded under wind and rain.
Yet let him mark this well: though garments fail,
Our spirits keep their edge. Before this sun
Has left the field, my men have sworn to me
They shall wear finer colours than they do now,
Or strip them from the backs of those opposed.
If fortune grants us victory, as I trust,
Then ransom will be gathered easily.
Return no more with offers or demands.
I pledge no payment save these weary limbs,
And if they fall into their enemies' hands,
The gain will hardly justify the trouble.
HERALD. I shall report your answer faithfully.
Farewell, good captain.
Exit Herald.
CAPTAIN. Yet I suspect we have not seen the last of him.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. My lord, I ask the honour of leading the first assault.
CAPTAIN. The honour is yours.
Now forward, gentlemen.
The day belongs to Heaven.
Exeunt.
SCENE IV. A Battlefield
Alarum. Enter RALPH, a captured Enemy Officer, and THOMAS, a young attendant.
RALPH. Surrender, rogue!
OFFICER. Good sir, have mercy.
RALPH. Mercy? That depends upon your purse.
What are you called?
OFFICER. I am Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain! Then your value rises.
Young Thomas, ask him whether he can pay.
THOMAS. Sir, my master wishes to know what ransom you offer.
OFFICER. Whatever is reasonable. Only spare my life.
RALPH. Hear him! He bargains already.
Tell him I am a fierce and dreadful warrior,
And not a man to be trifled with.
THOMAS. I shall tell him no such thing.
RALPH. Insolent boy! Do as you are bid.
THOMAS. Very well.
To Officer.
He says you should be frightened.
OFFICER. I am frightened enough.
RALPH. Good. Then mention a number.
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Two hundred?
The offer improves.
Ask whether he possesses more.
THOMAS. Do you?
OFFICER. Yes, if my family contributes.
RALPH. Excellent.
Inform him his life is spared.
THOMAS. He says he will release you.
OFFICER. Then I thank him sincerely.
I shall remember his generosity.
RALPH. What does he say?
THOMAS. He calls you generous.
RALPH. A just description.
Come, prisoner, follow me.
Exeunt Ralph and Officer.
THOMAS remains.
THOMAS. I have never witnessed so much noise proceeding from so little courage.
He boasts as though he had conquered kingdoms,
Yet trembles whenever real danger appears.
There are humbler men in the army
Who possess ten times his bravery
And not a fraction of his words.
Exit.

CAPTAIN. Carry this answer back to your commander.
Tell him we are soldiers, not courtiers.
Rain, mud, and hard marching have done their work on us.
The bright feathers are gone, the fine coats ruined,
and there is little splendour left in the army's appearance.
Still, appearances matter less than resolve.
My men believe that before night falls
they will either be dressed more richly than they are now,
or leave the field without need of clothing at all.
Should victory favour us,
ransom will take care of itself.
So save yourself another journey.
The only payment I promise is my own body,
and there is not much profit in that.
HERALD. I will deliver your words.
Exit.
CAPTAIN. Though I think he'll return before this business ends.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. Grant me the first advance, my lord.
CAPTAIN. Gladly.
Forward now.
Let Heaven determine the outcome.
They exit.

SCENE IV. Amid the Battle
Enter RALPH, dragging a captured officer. THOMAS follows.
RALPH. Yield yourself!
OFFICER. Sir, I surrender.
RALPH. Sensible at last.
Now tell me your name.
OFFICER. Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain? Then you must be worth keeping alive.
Thomas, ask him what his freedom is worth.
THOMAS. My master wishes to know your ransom.
OFFICER. I will pay what I can. Only spare me.
RALPH. You hear that? The man values life highly.
Tell him he stands before a warrior of fearsome reputation.
THOMAS. I doubt that will be necessary.
RALPH. Do as you're told.
THOMAS. As you wish.
To the Officer.
My master would like you to be impressed.
OFFICER. I am chiefly concerned with surviving.
RALPH. A practical fellow.
Well then, what sum?
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Better.
Perhaps there is more where that came from.
THOMAS. Is there?
OFFICER. My family could provide additional funds.
RALPH. Then I grow merciful.
Tell him he may keep his throat uncut.
THOMAS. He accepts your offer and grants your release.
OFFICER. I am deeply grateful.
RALPH. What gratitude does he express?
THOMAS. He believes himself fortunate.
RALPH. A wise judgment.
Come along.
Ralph leads the prisoner away.
THOMAS watches them go.
THOMAS. It is remarkable how loudly some men advertise their courage.
The fellow talks like a conqueror and bargains like a merchant.
I've known quieter soldiers who would face twice the danger with half the fuss.
The world often mistakes noise for bravery.
Exit.

“Enough of that,” he muttered. “We shall speak again.”
He left abruptly. A strange agitation possessed him—part triumph, part dread. His pulse raced; every nerve seemed strained. For a few moments he felt almost invigorated, as though some hidden spring of energy had been released. Yet the sensation faded almost immediately, leaving behind exhaustion so profound that each step became an effort.
The man he had left behind remained seated long after the conversation had ended. He stared at the table, absorbed in thought. Something in their exchange had unsettled him. A conclusion, vague before, now appeared obvious.
“No,” he said at last to himself. “That cannot be right. Everyone has judged him too hastily.”
Meanwhile, as Victor descended the stairs, he nearly collided with his friend Leon, who was rushing up them.
For several seconds they simply stared at one another.
“You!” Leon burst out. “So this is where you've been!”
His astonishment quickly gave way to anger.
“I've searched half the city. Do you hear me? Half the city! I went back to your room twice. I questioned your landlady. I nearly dragged your servant into the street demanding answers. And here you are strolling about as if nothing has happened.”
Victor looked at him wearily.
“I wanted to be left alone.”
“Alone?” Leon repeated. “You can barely stand. Look at yourself! You're pale as death.”
“Let me pass.”
“No.”
He seized Victor by the arm.
“You disappear without a word, you worry everyone who knows you, and then you tell me to let you pass?”
Victor's expression hardened.
“Why is it so difficult for you to understand? I do not want your concern. I did not ask for it. Every kindness feels like a chain. Every conversation exhausts me. You call it friendship. To me it feels like persecution.”
Leon released his arm, more from surprise than willingness.
Victor continued, speaking faster now.
“You all insist on helping. None of you stop to ask whether your help is wanted. Perhaps I would rather be miserable. Perhaps I would rather fail. Perhaps I simply wish to be left in peace. Why can no one accept that?”
His voice rose despite himself.
“You suffocate me. Every visit, every question, every expression of sympathy. Let me alone. For once, let me alone.”
The last words came out almost as a cry.
Leon stood silent.
Then, unexpectedly, his anger subsided.
“Well,” he said at length, “go where you please.”
Victor turned away.
“Wait.”
Leon pointed a finger at him.
“You people are all alike. Every disappointment becomes a tragedy. Every inconvenience becomes a philosophy. You lock yourselves inside your own heads and stare at your troubles until they grow enormous.”
Victor did not answer.
“No, listen. I mean it. If something hurts you, you polish the wound until it shines. Then you admire it. You call that depth. I call it vanity.”
His face reddened with the force of his irritation.
“And another thing. You're all determined to be original, yet you imitate one another perfectly. The same complaints. The same despair. The same speeches about being misunderstood.”
A reluctant smile almost crossed Victor's lips.
Leon noticed it and grew even louder.
“Don't laugh! I'm serious. This evening I'm hosting a gathering. My uncle is there already receiving guests because I ran out looking for you. If you had a grain of sense, you'd come. Sit among living people for once. Talk nonsense. Eat badly. Stay too late. Anything would be better than wandering the streets arguing with your own thoughts.”

“Enough. Until next time.”
With that he left.
The excitement that had carried him through the conversation still lingered, but only faintly. It was an uncomfortable feeling—something between exhilaration and illness. For a moment he walked quickly, almost energetically. Then the strength drained out of him again. His limbs felt heavy. Even breathing seemed laborious.
The other man remained behind, motionless.
He sat for a long while without touching the untouched glass before him. Their conversation had altered something in his thinking. A suspicion he had entertained for days now seemed less convincing.
“No,” he finally decided. “People have misunderstood him.”
As Victor stepped out into the street, he nearly ran straight into Leon.
The collision was so sudden that both stopped short.
Leon stared.
Then exploded.
“So I've found you at last!”
His voice echoed down the stairwell.
“Do you know where I've been looking? Everywhere! I went to your room. Twice. I questioned everyone who might have seen you. I was beginning to think you'd vanished completely.”
Victor looked exhausted.
“I asked for none of that.”
“Because you wanted to be alone?” Leon said incredulously. “You can hardly stay upright.”
“Move aside.”
Instead, Leon grabbed his shoulder.
“Not a chance.”
Victor's patience snapped.
“You call this friendship, but it feels like punishment. Can't you understand that? I don't want to be watched, advised, rescued, comforted. Every one of you arrives determined to improve my life. Has it occurred to you that I might not want improvement?”
The words came more quickly now.
“You never stop. Questions, concern, advice—endlessly. It wears me down. Leave me alone.”
He ended almost breathless.
Leon released him.
For a moment he seemed genuinely thoughtful.
“All right,” he said. “Go to the devil if that's your wish.”
Victor took a step.
“Not yet.”
Leon folded his arms.
“I've something to say first. The lot of you are impossible. You treat every personal difficulty as though it were a matter of world importance. You brood over your troubles day and night until they become your entire identity.”
Victor remained silent.
“Yes, that's right. Brood over them. Worship them. Feed them. Then congratulate yourselves on being profound.”
His irritation was returning rapidly.
“And don't imagine you're unique. You're all copies. Every one of you thinks the same gloomy thoughts and makes the same speeches about isolation and suffering.”
This time Victor almost smiled.
Leon caught it immediately.
“Don't mock me. I'm making perfect sense.”
He jabbed a finger toward the street.
“I'm having people over tonight. They've probably arrived already. My uncle is entertaining them while I chase you around the city. If you possessed even the smallest amount of wisdom, you'd come with me instead of wearing yourself into the pavement.”
He shook his head.
“Honestly, a few hours among ordinary human beings might do you more good than a month alone with your thoughts.”

Margaret had scarcely recovered from her surprise before another occasion for reflection presented itself. Mr. Hartwell, whose reserve had once appeared almost insurmountable, now conversed with her aunt and uncle with an ease that seemed entirely free of embarrassment. She had expected politeness, perhaps; but this degree of attention exceeded anything she had anticipated.
As her aunt introduced Mr. Benson as her brother, Margaret watched Mr. Hartwell closely. She could not resist observing whether the disclosure altered his manner. For an instant he appeared startled; yet the expression vanished almost as soon as it came. Instead of retreating into silence, he addressed Mr. Benson with renewed civility and soon engaged him in a discussion concerning the surrounding countryside.
Margaret listened with increasing satisfaction. Her uncle acquitted himself admirably. His observations were sensible, his remarks well chosen, and his good humour never descended into familiarity. Every answer he gave seemed to strengthen the favourable impression she hoped he might produce.
The gentlemen soon fell into conversation about improvements to the estate. Mr. Hartwell spoke at length of several recent alterations to the grounds and invited Mr. Benson to inspect them at his leisure. He even offered the use of certain maps and plans prepared by his surveyor, should they be of any interest.
Mrs. Benson exchanged a glance with her niece, expressing surprise without speaking. Margaret returned only a faint smile. She herself could scarcely account for what she witnessed.
Why had he changed so much? His former reserve had softened into something almost approaching openness. She could not persuade herself that such a transformation had occurred on her account. Their previous disagreements hardly seemed sufficient to produce so marked an alteration.
As they continued their walk, a slight rearrangement of the party occurred. Mrs. Benson, finding herself somewhat fatigued, accepted her husband's arm, leaving Margaret beside Mr. Hartwell. For a few moments neither spoke.
At length Margaret remarked that his presence had been wholly unexpected. She had understood, from several sources, that he was not expected at the estate until the following day.
Mr. Hartwell explained that business with his steward had required an earlier arrival than originally intended.
“The remainder of our party will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Among them are several persons already known to you.”
Margaret inclined her head but offered no immediate reply.
After a brief pause he continued.
“There is one among them, however, whose acquaintance I particularly hope you will make. My sister has heard much of you and would be pleased, I believe, to meet you during your stay here.”
The request took her entirely by surprise. She scarcely knew how to answer. Whatever desire his sister possessed to know her must surely have originated with him, and the thought was not without its comfort.
They walked on for some distance without speaking further. Margaret felt neither ease nor confidence, yet she could not deny a sense of gratification. Such an invitation implied a degree of regard she had not expected.
By the time they reached the carriage, the others had fallen considerably behind. Mr. Hartwell suggested that she might rest for a few minutes within the house, but she declined. The evening was pleasant, and they remained together upon the lawn, each occupied with thoughts not easily expressed.

Margaret had little time to dwell on her first surprise before another followed it. Mr. Hartwell, whose manners had once seemed guarded almost to coldness, was speaking with her aunt and uncle as though no awkwardness existed between them at all. She had expected him to be civil; she had not expected this.
When her aunt mentioned their connection to her, Margaret watched him almost involuntarily. She wondered whether the knowledge would alter his behaviour. If it did, the change was slight. A momentary look of surprise crossed his face, but it passed quickly. Rather than withdrawing, he turned again to her uncle and continued the conversation with evident interest.
The effect on Margaret was immediate. It pleased her more than she cared to admit. There was comfort in seeing that those nearest to her could meet his notice without causing embarrassment to either side.
She listened closely. Her uncle spoke with his usual good sense, easy humour, and quiet confidence. Whenever Mr. Hartwell responded warmly to one of his remarks, she felt a private satisfaction she could not entirely suppress.
The conversation drifted to the estate and the surrounding country. Mr. Hartwell pointed out several recent improvements and urged Mr. Benson to examine them whenever he wished. He offered plans of the grounds, spoke of future projects, and seemed genuinely eager to continue the discussion.
Mrs. Benson caught her niece's eye. The look she gave required no explanation.
Margaret, however, had none to offer. She was still asking herself the same question. What accounted for this difference? His reserve had not disappeared altogether, but it no longer governed every word and action. She could not persuade herself that she was the cause. The idea seemed too improbable.
Their party changed formation a short while later. Mrs. Benson, tired by the walk, took her husband's arm instead, and Margaret found herself beside Mr. Hartwell.
For several moments they walked in silence.
At last she remarked that his arrival had been unexpected. She had been assured he would not return until the next day.
He admitted as much and explained that business with his steward had brought him back sooner than planned.
“The others will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Some of them are already known to you.”
Margaret acknowledged this with a slight inclination of her head.
After a pause, he added, “There is another person among them whom I hope you will allow me to introduce. My sister has a strong wish to make your acquaintance while you remain here.”
The proposal startled her. For a second she could think of no answer at all.
Whatever interest his sister might have taken in her, it was impossible not to suspect the source. The thought carried a quiet satisfaction. At the very least, it suggested he did not view her with the unfavourable opinion she had once feared.
They continued on without much conversation. Margaret was not entirely at ease, but she was undeniably pleased. The invitation itself felt significant.
Before long they reached the carriage, well ahead of the others. Mr. Hartwell invited her to go inside and rest, but she declined. The air was mild, the evening calm. They remained on the lawn together, saying little, while each seemed occupied by thoughts better left unspoken.

His composure deserted him at last. I saw his hands tremble where they rested upon the arm of the chair, and though he strove to conceal it, grief betrayed itself in every movement. His eyes remained fixed upon the dying fire, yet it seemed to me he saw nothing before him. The room was silent save for the uneven sound of his breathing.
I watched him for some moments and, finding his misery a temptation too strong to resist, spoke with deliberate cruelty.
“You mourn her now,” I said, “yet you were never content while she lived.”
He lifted his head slowly. There was something in his expression that might once have warned me to silence, but suffering had dulled its force.
“Leave this house,” he answered.
The words were scarcely audible.
“I shall leave when I choose,” I replied. “Her memory gives me as much right to speak as it does you. She had kindness enough for all around her. Had she lived longer, she might have judged more clearly the worth of those who claimed to love her.”
He rose so abruptly that I stepped backward.
“Take care,” he said.
“Or what?” I returned. “Will you frighten me into agreement? No. I knew her better than you suppose. She would not have endured this bitterness forever. Whatever attachment bound her to you, it could not have survived unchanged.”
At that he seized a heavy book from the table and hurled it across the room. It struck the wall beside me and fell open at my feet.
The violence accomplished little beyond convincing me to depart.
I reached the doorway and turned once more.
“You are feared by many,” I said, “but fear is not devotion. Remember that.”
Then I fled.
Behind me I heard a crash of overturned furniture and a cry that sounded more wounded than angry. I did not pause to learn the cause. Down the passage, through the yard, and across the fields I ran, urged onward by equal measures of alarm and relief.
When at last the house disappeared behind the rising ground, I slowed my pace. The evening wind was cold, but never had open country seemed more welcoming. I would sooner have wandered the moors until morning than passed another hour beneath that roof.
Here the narrator paused. After a few moments she resumed in a calmer tone.
She left the district shortly afterward and settled many miles away among distant relations. Letters arrived from her occasionally, though they became less frequent as the years passed. News reached us that she had married again and borne a daughter, a delicate child who was said to resemble her mother closely.
As for the man she had fled, he remained unchanged in reputation, if not in spirit. From time to time he questioned those who had known her, asking where she lived and whether she prospered. Few answered willingly.
“Let her remain where she is,” he remarked once when I refused him information. “Distance serves us both.”
Yet he never spoke those words without a bitterness that suggested the opposite.
Years later, when her name was mentioned in his hearing, he would fall silent. Whatever else time had altered, it had not entirely subdued that old wound.

The effort to master himself was beginning to fail. Though he sat motionless by the fire, there was no mistaking the strain in him. His hands shook now and then, and his gaze remained fixed on the embers as if he expected them to answer him. The room had grown very still.
I watched longer than I should have. At last I spoke.
“You grieve for her now,” I said, “but you gave her little peace while she was here.”
He looked up. The expression might once have intimidated me; grief had worn it thin.
“Go,” he said quietly. “Leave me.”
“I will, in time.”
The answer escaped me before prudence could intervene. “But do not pretend you alone have the right to remember her. Others loved her too.”
For a moment he said nothing.
I continued, emboldened by his silence. “You speak as though devotion excuses everything. It does not. She saw more than you believed she did.”
That reached him.
He rose suddenly, and I retreated a step toward the door.
“Enough,” he said.
Still I persisted. “Had she lived, she might have judged you differently in the end.”
A book was the nearest object to his hand. He snatched it up and threw it. It struck the wall beside me with a force that ended the conversation more effectively than any argument.
I was already backing away.
At the threshold I turned once.
“People obey you because they fear you,” I said. “That is not the same thing.”
Then I was gone.
I heard furniture crash behind me, followed by a sound that was neither wholly rage nor wholly sorrow. I had no wish to discover which. Through the yard and across the fields I ran without stopping.
Only when the house had vanished behind the hills did I slow. The air was sharp and cold, but it felt like freedom. I would gladly have spent the night out on the moor rather than return.
The narrator rested for a moment before continuing.
Not long afterward she left the area altogether. She settled far away with relatives, and for a few years letters arrived with some regularity. Later they became rarer. We eventually learned she had remarried and had a daughter of fragile health, though by all accounts a cheerful disposition.
The man she left behind remained much the same to outward appearances. Occasionally he would ask after her. Sometimes he sought news indirectly, pretending little interest in the answer.
“Let her stay where she has chosen to be,” he said to me once.
The words sounded indifferent enough. Yet there was something in the way he spoke them that suggested indifference was the last thing he felt.
As the years passed, her name came up less often. When it did, he usually fell silent. Time had changed many things around him, but not that.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Now let them banquet where they praised me most,
And count their cups among their reckonings.
Not one remains whose hand would lift my burden,
Though all were eager when my board was spread.
The gold is gone, and with it goes their friendship.
I know them now.
Let every hall that echoed with their laughter
Stand empty as their promises. Let music
Forget the doors it once made glad. The guest
Who smiled upon my fortune and my table
Has vanished with the season of my wealth.
Yet I was blind.
I called these hollow men companions.
I measured hearts by bows and courteous phrases,
And purchased thanks that lasted but an hour.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we sought you through the city streets.
ARISTON.
Why seek me?
STEWARD.
Because we fear for you and for ourselves.
The house is left deserted. Creditors
Have sealed the gates. The servants wander idle,
Not knowing where to turn.
FIRST SERVANT.
Good master, speak.
The poorest among us grieves to see this change.
ARISTON.
Grieve not for me.
The world has shown its face at last.
I thank necessity for that instruction.
The flatterer has fled; the borrower forgets;
The friend who swore eternal faith remembers
No syllable of all his former vows.
SECOND SERVANT.
There are some honest men.
ARISTON.
If so, they hide.
I searched for one and found a hundred masks.
The city breeds fair words and barren deeds.
Its marketplaces traffic not in goods alone,
But in affection, honour, gratitude.
STEWARD.
Will you not stay and make your peace with fortune?
ARISTON.
No. Fortune and I are strangers now.
Let those who worship her attend her court.
I shall go where the woods ask nothing of me,
Where no man praises what he means to steal.
FIRST SERVANT.
Then we will follow.
ARISTON.
No.
Keep what remains of your poor livelihoods.
I go alone.
STEWARD.
The gods protect you, sir.
ARISTON.
If they have watched in silence until now,
They may continue so. Farewell to all.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
A noble heart brought low.
SECOND SERVANT.
And lower still by those who fed upon it.
STEWARD.
Come. We must gather what the ruin leaves.
When wealth departs, companions vanish first;
Yet honest sorrow lingers after both.
Exeunt.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Let them feast without me.
The same men who crowded round my table will scarcely spare a thought now that my coffers are empty. While the wine flowed, I was their dearest friend. Now the gold is spent, and so are their affections.
How readily I believed them.
Every hall that once rang with greetings and laughter seems different in memory. I mistook noise for loyalty. A pleasant word, a flattering smile, a show of concern—these passed for friendship in my eyes.
No longer.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we have searched everywhere for you.
ARISTON.
And why?
STEWARD.
The house is in disorder. Creditors press their claims. The gates are watched, the servants uncertain what is to become of them. We feared you had left us altogether.
FIRST SERVANT.
It troubles us deeply to see your fortunes changed so suddenly.
ARISTON.
Do not waste pity on me. What has happened has at least taught me something. Prosperity gathered people around me; adversity has shown me who they were.
The borrower forgets. The flatterer disappears. Those who swore they loved me cannot even remember the words they spoke.
SECOND SERVANT.
Surely not everyone is false.
ARISTON.
Perhaps not. Yet I looked for sincerity and found performance. This city values appearances above all things. Gratitude is traded away as easily as coin.
STEWARD.
What will you do now?
ARISTON.
Leave.
Let others chase favour and fortune. I have no appetite for either. The forests are more honest than crowded streets. Trees ask nothing, promise nothing, and deceive no one.
FIRST SERVANT.
Allow us to come with you.
ARISTON.
No. You must think of yourselves. Keep what work you can find and make better use of trust than I did.
STEWARD.
Then may heaven watch over you.
ARISTON.
Heaven has been a distant observer. Still, farewell.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
I never thought I would see him brought to this.
SECOND SERVANT.
His wealth failed him; his friends failed him sooner.
STEWARD.
Come. There is still much to set in order. When fortune leaves a house, it takes most of its visitors with it. What remains is seldom comfort, but sometimes it is truth.
Exeunt.

For if these things be defended under the name of religion, they furnish the strongest evidence against those who defend them. For men are not wholly deprived of judgment, however much habit and corruption may cloud their minds. Even among those most given to vice there remains some faint admiration for virtue. Therefore the authors of deception, if they wish to ensnare the many, must borrow the appearance of what is good. They praise temperance in words and reward excess in practice; they commend humility with their lips while exalting pride before the eyes of the multitude.
Thus the crowd is taught by spectacle rather than by doctrine. Few listen to sober instruction, but many delight in public display. The result is that honorable precepts are spoken quietly to small assemblies, while disgraceful examples are exhibited openly and celebrated. The lesson most eagerly learned is not the one that is taught, but the one that is applauded.
Consider the festivals that are instituted in honor of divine powers. The people gather in great numbers. Songs are sung, ceremonies are performed, and sacred names are invoked. Yet amid these observances are introduced entertainments whose purpose is not the cultivation of virtue but the encouragement of passion. Those who attend are told that such things are acceptable to heaven. What wonder, then, if they return home with weakened discipline and corrupted desires?
For men are more readily persuaded by what they see than by what they hear. If modesty is praised while shamelessness is displayed, the display triumphs over the praise. If restraint is commended while indulgence is honored before the public eye, indulgence becomes the true object of imitation. The multitude does not inquire what is best; it follows what is celebrated.
What spirit, then, delights in such contradictions? What power urges men toward vice while pretending concern for their improvement? Surely no friend of righteousness would seek to heal conduct by means that inflame corruption. Such practices resemble a physician who commends health while secretly administering poison.
Hence it is not surprising that commonwealths decline when they mistake moral decay for religious devotion. Citizens labor to appease supernatural powers through ceremonies, yet neglect the virtues by which societies are preserved. They fear offending the gods by omitting festivals, but do not fear offending justice through greed, luxury, and dishonesty.
In this way the foundations of public order are gradually weakened. The walls of a city may stand firm, its armies may remain numerous, and its treasuries full; yet if virtue is abandoned, ruin has already entered within the gates. No enemy is more dangerous than the corruption that grows under the protection of sacred approval.

If such customs are defended as acts of religion, they become an argument against themselves. Human beings do not entirely lose their sense of right and wrong, even when vice has become familiar. However corrupt a society may be, people still recognize virtue when they encounter it. Because of this, those who wish to mislead others rarely present evil in its true form. They dress it in respectable language. Temperance is praised, while excess is tolerated. Humility is admired in speeches, but pride receives the applause.
The greater influence comes not from what is taught but from what is displayed. A quiet lesson reaches a few listeners; a public spectacle reaches thousands. When honorable principles are confined to words and dishonorable conduct is turned into entertainment, the example outweighs the instruction. People remember what they see celebrated.
One may observe this in public festivals. Crowds gather, sacred ceremonies are performed, and divine names are invoked with solemnity. Yet alongside these rites appear amusements that excite vanity, appetite, and passion. Those attending are encouraged to regard such things as acceptable, even admirable. It should surprise no one if they leave less disciplined than when they arrived.
Human beings learn powerfully through imitation. If modesty is praised but shamelessness is put on display, the display leaves the deeper impression. If self-restraint is commended while indulgence is rewarded with attention and admiration, indulgence becomes the model that many follow. The public example quietly defeats the spoken rule.
What sort of spirit delights in this arrangement? Certainly not one devoted to goodness. No sincere guide would encourage corruption while claiming to improve character. It is like offering poison under the name of medicine.
Nor should we wonder when societies suffer the consequences. People become diligent in ceremonies yet careless about virtue. They fear neglecting a festival more than neglecting justice. They concern themselves with outward observances while greed, dishonesty, and excess spread unchecked.
A nation may possess strong defenses, wealthy institutions, and every appearance of stability. Yet when moral discipline decays, the danger is already within. External enemies can do great harm, but the corruption that receives public approval is often more destructive than any army at the gates.

She hesitated at first, for the two extraordinary creatures had drawn so near that their noses almost touched her sleeves. One blinked solemnly; the other smiled without appearing to know why. Still, finding neither intended to bite, she gathered confidence and continued her account.
Both listeners remained silent until she reached the part concerning the upside-down clock that had insisted upon striking tomorrow before today. Then the larger creature gave a long whistle.
“That is remarkably peculiar,” he observed.
“Peculiar?” cried the smaller one. “It is peculiarity itself!”
“The clock struck tomorrow?” repeated the larger creature thoughtfully. “I should like to hear something else. Let her recite.”
He nodded toward his companion, who immediately assumed an air of authority.
“Stand up and repeat ‘The Busy Bee,’” he commanded.
Alice was not at all pleased.
“They are forever ordering people about,” she thought. “One would imagine lessons were the principal amusement of the world.”
Nevertheless she rose and began. Unfortunately her thoughts were still occupied with the clock, and the verse emerged in a very altered form indeed:
“The curious Bee sat under the sea,
And polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat,
And dined upon lavender ham.”
The larger creature shook his head.
“That is not how it goes.”
“No,” agreed the smaller one, “though I cannot say how it ought to go either.”
Alice sat down quickly, feeling rather embarrassed.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I do not know,” said Alice.
“Then why say it?”
“I was told to.”
This answer appeared reasonable to her, though neither of the creatures seemed satisfied.
“Try another verse,” said the smaller one.
Alice sighed.
“Beyond the hill where the teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, but the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
The larger creature looked alarmed.
“Can minnows carry buttons?”
“Only on formal occasions,” said the smaller one.
Before Alice could protest, both creatures began arguing over the matter. Their disagreement grew louder and more complicated until neither appeared to remember what they were discussing.
At length the larger creature stopped.
“Enough of verses,” he declared. “Let us have a song.”
“A song!” cried the other. “A song at once!”
Without waiting for permission, he began:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Revolving slowly through the sky!
Who would not trade a crown or two
For one small slice of splendid Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He paused dramatically, then continued in an even louder voice:
“O glorious Pie, of berry and breeze,
Balanced with effortless expertise!
Whether it whistles or whether it sings,
Pie is the finest of edible things!”
“Again!” cried the larger creature.
The singer drew breath to begin once more, but at that moment a distant voice rang across the field:
“The inspection is starting!”
Immediately both creatures sprang to their feet.
“The inspection!” they shouted together, and dashed away, leaving Alice more puzzled than ever.

At first she felt a little uneasy. The two strange creatures had edged so close that she could hear one of them breathing through its nose. The other stared at her with such interest that she wondered whether she had accidentally become part of the entertainment.
Still, they seemed harmless enough, so she went on with her story.
They listened without interruption until she described the clock that insisted on announcing tomorrow before today. That detail produced a strong reaction.
“Well, that is odd,” said the larger creature after a moment.
“Odd?” said the smaller. “It is wonderfully odd.”
“The clock truly did that?”
Alice nodded.
“I should like to hear her recite something,” the larger creature decided.
His companion immediately pointed at her.
“Stand up and say a poem.”
Alice thought this extremely unfair. Everyone she met seemed determined to assign her lessons.
Even so, she stood and tried to comply. The trouble was that her mind was still occupied with impossible clocks, and the poem emerged rather differently than intended:
“The curious Bee beneath the sea
Polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat
And feasted on lavender ham.”
“That sounds wrong,” remarked the larger creature.
“It certainly sounds unusual,” said the smaller.
Alice sat down again.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I have no idea.”
“Then why recite it?”
“Because you asked me to.”
Neither creature seemed able to argue with that, though both looked disappointed.
“Another verse,” said the smaller one.
With a sigh, Alice continued:
“Beyond the hill where teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, and the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
“Can a minnow carry buttons?” the larger creature wondered aloud.
“On special occasions, certainly,” replied the smaller.
This led to a debate so lengthy and so confusing that they soon forgot what had started it. When at last the discussion collapsed under its own weight, the larger creature announced that poetry had been exhausted and that music was required.
“A song!” cried the smaller creature enthusiastically.
Without further ceremony he launched into one:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Drifting across the sky!
Who would not trade a treasure chest
For one delightful bite of Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He was preparing to repeat the chorus when a distant shout interrupted him.
“The inspection is starting!”
The effect was immediate. Both creatures leapt up.
“The inspection!” they cried.
And before Alice could ask what was being inspected, they had vanished, leaving her alone and no nearer to understanding anything than she had been before.

The vessel pressed onward through the long heaving swells, cutting the ocean with a steady violence that sent spray flying from her bows. Every spar groaned with the strain, and yet the ship seemed eager for the pursuit, as though some hidden desire possessed her timbers. The men moved about the decks with restless expectation. Each glance cast toward the horizon carried the same question, and each moment of delay sharpened it.
“There again! There again! Off the larboard bow!” came the cry from above.
The words passed through the crew like a spark through dry grass. Heads turned at once. Hands tightened upon ropes and rails. Even those who had spoken little during the morning now stared ahead with shining eyes.
The captain stood unmoving for a moment. Then he stepped forward and fixed his gaze upon the distant sea.
“Keep her course,” he said. “Let no man lose sight of that place.”
The ocean before them appeared empty save for a patch of white disturbance far ahead. Yet all knew the sign. A whale had broken the surface and vanished again beneath the dark water.
Excitement spread among them. Old fears, doubts, and warnings were forgotten. The long chase, the weary watches, the uncertain days behind them—all seemed justified by that fleeting glimpse. Their purpose narrowed to a single point upon the sea.
The ship rushed onward. Wind filled the sails until they swelled like great clouds overhead. The masts bent slightly under the pressure, and the deck trembled beneath the continual drive forward.
Again the lookout shouted.
This time there could be no mistake.
The whale erupted from the depths in a vast arching leap. Water cascaded from its shining body. For an instant it hung against the sky, immense and magnificent, before crashing back into the sea with a report like distant artillery. Foam burst outward in every direction.
A great cry rose from the ship.
The crew surged toward the rails. Some pointed; others laughed aloud. The sight overwhelmed caution. The creature seemed less an animal than a force of nature, appearing suddenly from the hidden world below and declaring its presence to all who watched.
The captain’s eyes never left it.
“There is our quarry,” he said quietly.
The men heard him and answered with renewed energy. Whatever fate awaited them beyond that horizon, they were committed now. Ship, sea, wind, and crew had become joined in a single pursuit, driving after the mighty shape that moved before them through the endless blue.

The ship drove hard across the swells, her bow throwing up sheets of spray as she carved through the water. Every beam and spar complained under the strain, but there was something eager in her motion. She seemed to lean into the chase. Around the deck the crew worked with growing impatience, their attention returning again and again to the horizon.
A cry rang down from the masthead.
“There! Off the larboard bow!”
At once the mood changed. Men stopped what they were doing. A few stepped to the rail. Others shaded their eyes and searched the distance. The call had awakened the same hope in all of them.
The captain looked ahead without speaking. After a brief pause he gave his order.
“Hold the course. Keep your eyes on that water.”
Far off, a patch of white turbulence marked the sea. It was small enough to vanish if one looked away for a moment, yet everyone aboard understood what it meant. Something immense had surfaced and slipped below again.
The sight was enough. Days of uncertainty faded from memory. Fatigue no longer mattered. The chase, which had stretched on through long watches and empty horizons, suddenly felt alive again.
Wind pressed heavily into the sails. The vessel answered, surging forward. The rigging hummed, the masts flexed, and a faint shudder ran beneath every footstep on deck.
Then the lookout shouted once more.
This time nobody doubted.
The whale burst from the sea in a tremendous leap. Water streamed from its body as it rose, suspended for a heartbeat against the bright sky. Then it crashed down. The impact rolled across the water like cannon fire, and a wide circle of foam exploded around it.
A roar went up from the crew.
Men pointed and called to one another. Some laughed from sheer excitement. The creature seemed almost unreal—less a living thing than a sudden manifestation of the ocean itself.
The captain watched in silence.
At last he spoke.
“There is the one we seek.”
His words carried across the deck. No speech was needed after that. Every man knew his part. Drawn together by the same purpose, ship and crew pressed on through the wind and sunlight, following the dark shape moving ahead across the open sea.

“I assure you, there is no mistake,” said Marianne. “I have never spoken with greater sincerity. He has declared himself, and I have accepted him.”
Her sister stared in astonishment. “You cannot expect me to believe it immediately. Only a short time ago, you could scarcely mention his name with patience.”
“That belongs entirely to the past,” replied Marianne. “If I judged him harshly before, I was mistaken. I see him differently now. Indeed, I think it would be unjust to dwell upon former opinions when everything has so completely changed.”
Still her sister seemed uncertain.
“You are serious?” she asked at last. “Perfectly serious?”
“Perfectly. I would not trifle with you on such a subject.”
“My dear Marianne!” she cried, taking her hand. “Then I wish you every happiness. Yet tell me honestly—are you certain of your own feelings? Do you esteem him enough? Do you love him as you ought?”
“I do. More than I imagined possible.”
“Then I am content,” said her sister. “Nothing else is necessary. But you have concealed this wonderfully well. Looking back, I can now understand many things that puzzled me, though at the time I suspected nothing.”
“There was little to tell at first,” said Marianne. “Even to myself, the change was gradual. I did not know my own heart until quite recently.”
They continued talking for some time, reviewing conversations and incidents that now appeared in a different light. What had once seemed insignificant acquired fresh meaning. By the end of their discussion, every doubt had disappeared.
The following morning, their mother observed from the drawing-room window that two gentlemen were approaching the house.
“There they are again!” she exclaimed. “I declare, they seem determined to spend every day here. Well, I cannot complain, for one of them is always welcome. But the other surprises me by his persistence.”
Marianne coloured slightly and turned away.
A few moments later the visitors entered. One addressed her sister with unusual warmth, while the other approached Marianne with a composure that concealed, though only imperfectly, his satisfaction.
Their mother, entirely unaware of the true state of affairs, soon proposed an excursion.
“The weather is delightful,” she said. “You should all walk to the hill beyond the meadow. The view is particularly fine this time of year.”
Her younger daughter immediately declined.
“I would rather remain at home,” she said.
“Then the rest of you must go without her,” replied their mother. “There is no reason everyone should lose the pleasure.”
The suggestion was received with more approval than she realized, and several exchanged glances that were not wholly accidental.

“There is nothing uncertain about it,” said Marianne. “I mean every word. He asked me to marry him, and I agreed.”
Her sister looked at her for a long moment.
“Marianne, I can hardly comprehend it. Not very long ago you spoke of him with so little favour.”
Marianne smiled.
“Then you must forget what I said before. I have done so myself. People change their opinions when they learn more of one another. At least, I have.”
The explanation did not entirely remove her sister’s surprise.
“You truly mean it?”
“Entirely.”
“My dear Marianne,” she said, squeezing her hand, “then I am happy for you. But allow me one question. Are you quite certain this attachment is strong enough to make you happy?”
“It is stronger than I ever expected it could be.”
That answer seemed to satisfy her.
“Then I have no further concerns. Still, you have been remarkably secretive. I see now that there were signs, but I never understood them.”
“I scarcely understood them myself,” Marianne admitted. “The change happened so gradually that I cannot point to any exact moment when it began.”
The conversation continued well into the evening. Together they revisited recent events, discovering meanings that had escaped them at the time. By the end, there was little left unexplained.
The next morning their mother happened to glance from the drawing-room window.
“Well!” she cried. “Here come our visitors again.”
She watched them approach the house with evident amusement.
“One might think they had no other destination. Not that I object. Their company is always agreeable enough.”
Marianne lowered her eyes, hoping no one would notice the colour rising in her cheeks.
When the gentlemen entered, greetings were exchanged all around. One spoke with particular warmth to her sister, while the other directed most of his attention toward Marianne, though with studied restraint.
Before long their mother proposed a walk.
“You should all go to the hill beyond the meadow,” she said. “The air is pleasant, and the view is worth seeing.”
Her younger daughter immediately shook her head.
“No, thank you. I would rather stay here.”
“Then stay,” said her mother. “The rest need not be deprived of an outing on your account.”
The proposal appeared perfectly innocent. Yet the quiet looks exchanged across the room suggested that some among the party found it especially welcome.

“You must not remain idle,” said the doctor. “A settled routine, some occupation, a purpose that carries you forward—those things would do you more good than any medicine I could prescribe.”
“Yes, perhaps you are right,” replied Nikolai after a pause. “I shall return to my studies soon. Once I am occupied again, everything may become simpler.”
The doctor nodded with satisfaction, though he was secretly disappointed. He had expected gratitude, perhaps even admiration for his advice. Instead, when he looked closely at his patient, he thought he detected a trace of irony in his expression. The impression vanished almost immediately.
His mother hastened to fill the silence.
“We owe you so much already,” she said warmly. “Especially after your kindness in visiting us last night.”
“You saw them last night?” Nikolai asked, turning toward the doctor. “Then you sacrificed your rest as well.”
“It was nothing of consequence,” said the doctor. “I was awake regardless.”
Nikolai lowered his eyes.
“To tell the truth, I do not understand why everyone troubles themselves on my account. You have all shown me more concern than I deserve. I ought to be grateful, yet I find myself embarrassed by it. It is difficult to accept what I cannot explain.”
“You are overthinking the matter,” said the doctor with a laugh. “Young physicians become attached to their early patients. We imagine ourselves responsible for them. Besides, I have not yet the luxury of being overwhelmed with work.”
“And he,” Nikolai added, indicating his friend, “has received nothing in return for his efforts except inconvenience.”
“What absurdity!” his friend exclaimed. “You speak as though we are strangers.”
His sister, however, watched him carefully. There was something in his manner that unsettled her. His words were kind, but they seemed rehearsed, as if he had prepared them beforehand.
He turned toward his mother.
“As for you, I only realized today how much anxiety I caused. You must have suffered greatly yesterday.”
Then, unexpectedly, he extended his hand toward his sister.
The gesture was simple, but the feeling behind it appeared genuine. She grasped his hand immediately. Relief brightened her face. Their disagreement of the previous day seemed suddenly less important.
The mother's eyes filled with tears.
“There,” she thought, “that is the son I know. Whatever troubles him, his heart remains good.”
Her gaze lingered on him. He looked exhausted. His clothes were worn, his appearance neglected. Yet to her he remained dearer than anyone in the world.
Suddenly she began speaking again.
“You cannot imagine how frightened we were yesterday. We arrived full of hope and were told at once that you had left in a feverish state. They were searching the streets for you. I thought of every terrible possibility. I scarcely knew what to do.”
The memory still shook her.
“For hours I could think of nothing else. Every story of illness or misfortune returned to me. I feared we might lose you before we had even seen you properly again.”
The room fell silent. No one doubted the sincerity of her distress.

“You need something to occupy you,” said the doctor. “Work, study, any clear purpose. A man recovers faster when he has somewhere to direct his thoughts.”
Nikolai gave a faint nod.
“Yes. I suppose that is true. I will return to the university before long. Perhaps once I fall back into a routine, things will seem less complicated.”
The doctor appeared pleased by the answer, though only for a moment. When he glanced at his patient, he thought he caught a hint of amusement—or perhaps mockery—in his face. It disappeared so quickly that he could not be certain.
His mother intervened before the silence became awkward.
“We are deeply grateful to you,” she said. “Especially for calling on us last evening.”
Nikolai looked up.
“You visited them last night? Then you have not had much rest yourself.”
“It was no great sacrifice,” the doctor replied lightly.
Nikolai frowned and looked down at his hands.
“What puzzles me is why everyone concerns themselves so much with me. I know I ought to be thankful. Still, I cannot quite understand it. The kindness itself becomes a burden when I feel I have done nothing to earn it.”
The doctor laughed.
“There is no mystery. Young doctors become attached to their first patients. We take an unreasonable interest in them. And, to be honest, I am not so busy that I can afford indifference.”
Nikolai gestured toward his friend.
“And he has endured even more. Trouble, inconvenience, endless demands—nothing else.”
“Nonsense,” his friend said at once. “You speak as though I have been performing a great service.”
His sister remained quiet. She had been watching him all this time. There was warmth in what he said, yet something restrained beneath it, something she could not quite name.
After a moment he turned to his mother.
“I understand now how worried you must have been yesterday. At least a little. I did not think of it before.”
Then he reached out his hand to his sister.
Nothing dramatic accompanied the gesture, but it was enough. She took his hand immediately, her face brightening with relief. The quarrel that had stood between them seemed to dissolve without another word.
Their mother could hardly contain her happiness.
For a few moments she simply looked at him. His clothes were shabby, his face drawn from fatigue, and yet she found herself thinking how dear he was to her. No outward disorder could alter that.
The thought stirred her into speech once more.
“You have no idea what yesterday was like for us. We arrived eager to see you and learned almost at once that you had gone out while ill. They said you were wandering about in a fever. People were searching everywhere.”
She paused, remembering.
“I imagined every possible disaster. The longer you were gone, the worse my fears became. I do not think I have ever passed such anxious hours.”
No one interrupted her. The depth of her distress was evident, and the room grew quiet as she spoke.

The decision to travel had been mine alone, though Clara consented to it with visible reluctance. She feared less for the hardships of the road than for the solitude into which I willingly cast myself. Her concern had procured for me the companionship of Ashton, whose cheerful temper she believed might draw me from my habitual gloom. Yet no kindness could wholly overcome the burden that oppressed my thoughts.
When at last I departed, she stood silent beside the gate, her composure maintained only by effort. I attempted words of reassurance, but they died before reaching my lips. The carriage bore me away through valleys bright with summer light, across hills clothed in forests, and beside rivers that reflected the clear sky. These scenes passed before me almost unnoticed. My mind remained fixed upon the object of my journey and the unfinished labor that awaited me.
Several days later I arrived at a small city upon the frontier and there remained until Ashton joined me. The contrast between us was striking. Every novelty delighted him. He spoke with enthusiasm of distant towers, crowded marketplaces, and the varied character of the people we encountered. Where I saw only passing forms, he discovered endless occasions for admiration.
“You observe nothing,” he said one evening as we walked beside the river. “The world spreads its treasures before you, yet you move among them as though in a dream.”
I could not deny the justice of his remark. The beauty that animated him seemed unable to penetrate the cloud that surrounded me. Nevertheless, I listened while he described the changing colors of the sunset and the reflections trembling upon the water. His pleasure was sincere and infectious, and at moments I almost believed myself capable of sharing it.
We continued our route by boat, allowing the current to carry us through a succession of fertile districts. Vineyards descended the hillsides in ordered rows. Ancient fortresses crowned rocky heights and overlooked the winding river below. In some places the landscape appeared severe and lonely; in others it opened suddenly into broad fields dotted with villages and church spires.
The season of harvest had begun. Along the banks we heard laborers singing as they worked, their voices carried faintly across the water. Children ran among the orchards, and carts laden with fruit moved slowly along the roads. Such signs of ordinary happiness awakened in me a feeling I had not experienced for many months. Though my anxieties remained, they seemed less absolute than before.
One afternoon, as our vessel drifted beneath a range of wooded cliffs, I reclined against the railing and watched clouds gathering in the western sky. The air grew cooler, and a gentle wind stirred the surface of the river. For a brief interval my thoughts loosened their hold upon me. I ceased to dwell upon the future and surrendered myself to the quiet motion of the voyage.
Ashton, meanwhile, appeared enchanted by everything around him. He spoke of distant countries he hoped to visit and of the many wonders still hidden beyond the horizon. His imagination transformed every scene into the beginning of some greater adventure. Observing his delight, I could not help envying the ease with which he embraced the world.
“Perhaps,” he said as evening descended, “the greatest privilege is not to discover new lands, but to retain the ability to wonder at them.”
The remark lingered with me long after the light had faded and the river had vanished into darkness.

The journey was my own idea, though Clara agreed to it reluctantly. What troubled her was not the travel itself but the thought of my being left alone with my thoughts. She had arranged for Ashton to accompany me, hoping his good spirits would keep me from sinking further into melancholy. It was a thoughtful plan, though at the time I doubted anyone could lighten the mood in which I found myself.
On the morning of my departure she tried to appear calm. A few words passed between us, neither of us saying quite what we meant. Then I was gone. The road led through pleasant country—wooded hills, bright rivers, fields warmed by the sun—but I paid little attention to any of it. My mind was occupied elsewhere, fixed on the purpose that had drawn me from home.
I waited several days at a frontier town before Ashton arrived. From the moment we resumed our travels together, the difference between us became impossible to ignore. Everything interested him. A marketplace, a ruined tower, even a crowded ferry landing could hold his attention for an hour. He had a way of finding something worth admiring wherever he looked.
One evening, while we walked beside the river, he laughed and told me I passed through the world half asleep.
“There is beauty everywhere,” he said. “You look directly at it and never seem to see it.”
The criticism was fair. I had become so accustomed to my own worries that I scarcely noticed what surrounded me. Still, as he pointed out the fading colors in the sky and the reflections scattered across the water, I found myself listening with unexpected interest.
We traveled onward by boat. The river wound through fertile country where vineyards climbed the slopes and old stone fortresses stood watch above the banks. Sometimes the scenery was wild and austere, marked by cliffs and dark forests. A bend in the river would reveal an entirely different prospect—open fields, villages, and church spires rising from among clusters of trees.
Harvest season was underway. Songs drifted from the fields. We saw wagons heavy with fruit and workers gathering crops beneath the late afternoon sun. There was something reassuring in these ordinary scenes. For the first time in many months, I felt a faint easing of the pressure that had weighed upon me.
One afternoon clouds gathered over the western hills as we floated beneath a line of wooded heights. The breeze freshened, rippling the surface of the river. Leaning against the railing, I watched the changing light and, for a little while, forgot to dwell on what lay ahead. The relief was brief, but it was real.
Ashton remained delighted by everything. Every town suggested a story, every distant road another destination. He spoke eagerly of future travels and places he hoped one day to see. Listening to him, I envied the openness with which he met the world.
As darkness settled over the water, he made a remark that stayed with me.
“People spend their lives searching for wonders,” he said. “The rarer gift may be never losing the ability to be surprised by them.”
The river disappeared into the night, but those words remained long after the last light had gone.

When armed conflict swept rapidly across the region, members of a national youth sports program found themselves confronting a reality they had never imagined. Training sessions ended overnight. Team facilities were abandoned. Athletes erased online profiles, destroyed documents linking them to international organizations, and sought refuge with relatives or trusted friends.
Within days, a small network of coaches, human rights advocates, former athletes, and volunteers began working across multiple countries to coordinate an evacuation effort. The challenge was immense. Communications were unreliable, transportation routes shifted constantly, and security conditions deteriorated by the hour.
For many of those involved, the mission became personal. Several coaches had spent years mentoring the athletes and had watched them become symbols of opportunity and social change in communities where such opportunities had long been limited. What began as a sports initiative had gradually evolved into something much larger: a platform for education, leadership, and independence.
“The team represented far more than competition,” one former coach explained. “For many of these young people, sport opened doors that had previously been closed. It gave them confidence, visibility, and a sense that their futures could be different.”
As reports of violence increased, organizers worked around the clock. Volunteers in different time zones exchanged information continuously, assembling lists, arranging documentation, and searching for safe routes. Many slept only a few hours each night while monitoring developments and responding to urgent messages.
The athletes themselves faced extraordinary risks. Some traveled long distances through crowded checkpoints. Others waited for days with limited food, water, and shelter. Families were forced to make difficult decisions about whether to remain together or separate temporarily in hopes that at least some members could reach safety.
Eventually, after a series of complex negotiations involving government agencies, nonprofit organizations, and international partners, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were evacuated from the country. Images confirming their departure brought an outpouring of relief among those who had worked to help them.
Yet the successful operation was accompanied by mixed emotions. While many had escaped immediate danger, others remained behind. Organizers described feelings of gratitude, exhaustion, and guilt, questioning whether more could have been done for those still at risk.
The uncertainty did not end with evacuation. The athletes now faced the challenge of rebuilding their lives in unfamiliar places, adapting to new cultures, languages, and educational systems. Support organizations began coordinating housing, counseling, and long-term resettlement assistance.
Despite the difficulties ahead, those closest to the team expressed confidence in their resilience. Years of overcoming obstacles had prepared them for adversity, even on a scale few could have anticipated.
“These young people have already shown extraordinary courage,” said one advocate. “The circumstances have changed, but their determination has not. The next chapter of their lives may begin in a different country, but the qualities that carried them this far remain the same.”
For many participants in the rescue effort, the experience left a lasting impression. They spoke not only of the lives saved, but also of the responsibility they felt toward communities whose futures had suddenly become uncertain. Long after the headlines faded, they knew the work would continue.

The collapse came so quickly that many athletes barely had time to react. One week they were attending training sessions and planning future competitions; the next they were hiding from view, deleting online accounts, and destroying anything that connected them to international organizations.
As conditions worsened, an informal group of coaches, former players, volunteers, and human rights advocates began trying to get them out. They were scattered across different countries and time zones, communicating through phones and laptops, often with incomplete information. None of them knew whether the effort would succeed.
For the people involved, this was never just about sport. Many had worked with the athletes for years. They had seen firsthand how participation in organized athletics created opportunities for education, employment, and personal freedom. The teams had become symbols of possibility in places where such opportunities were often hard to find.
“Sport was the starting point,” one coach said. “What really mattered was what came with it—confidence, independence, and the belief that young people could shape their own futures.”
The rescue effort quickly turned into a race against time. Volunteers stayed awake deep into the night coordinating paperwork, verifying names, and tracking changing security conditions. Messages arrived constantly. Some carried hope; others brought news of new obstacles.
Meanwhile, the athletes faced dangers of their own. Several spent days moving between temporary shelters. Families waited in crowded areas with limited supplies, uncertain whether they would be allowed to continue. Every decision carried consequences, and none of the options felt safe.
After days of uncertainty, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were finally evacuated through a complex operation involving governments, aid organizations, and international partners. When photographs arrived showing them safely aboard departing flights, many of the organizers were overcome with emotion.
Relief, however, was only part of the story.
The people who helped coordinate the evacuation spoke openly about exhaustion and guilt. Even after a successful outcome, they remained focused on those who had not been able to leave. Several described a lingering sense of responsibility that did not disappear once the flights were airborne.
The athletes themselves now faced a different challenge: starting over. New countries offered safety, but also unfamiliar languages, cultures, schools, and communities. Support groups immediately began helping with housing, counseling, and long-term settlement plans.

Team Asia secured a dramatic victory in the International Women's Golf Challenge on Sunday after edging Team North America 14.5-13.5 at Seaside Links in California. The decisive moment came when rookie golfer Hana Kim calmly sank a six-foot putt on the final hole to defeat American veteran Brooke Jensen, sealing her team's first title defense in the prestigious tournament that brings together top female golfers from across the globe.
The victory was confirmed shortly afterward when teammate Yuki Tanaka halved the final match, prompting emotional celebrations among the Asian squad. Team captain Mi-Young Park was embraced by players and staff as the significance of the achievement became clear. Park, who had guided the team to victory two years earlier, became the first captain in the competition's history to win consecutive titles.
Speaking after the event, Park said she was proud of her players and hinted that this would likely be her final time leading the team. "We've built something special together," she told reporters. "There are many talented former players who could do this role well, and I think it's time for someone else to have the opportunity. These experiences have meant a great deal to me."
Messages of congratulations quickly appeared online from players and officials around the world. Among them was men's team captain David Reynolds, whose squad will compete in an international event later this month. "An incredible display of determination and teamwork," Reynolds wrote on social media.
The final day began with Team Asia holding an 8.5-7.5 lead and needing six points from the singles matches to retain the trophy. The visitors started strongly, winning three of the opening four contests and increasing their advantage. However, Team North America responded with a series of impressive performances, collecting points in several consecutive matches and narrowing the gap as the competition entered its closing stages.
With thousands of spectators lining the fairways and tension building throughout the afternoon, Kim delivered the shot that ultimately secured victory. The 24-year-old had only recently earned her place on the professional circuit after winning a regional tournament in South Korea earlier in the summer.
"I can't really believe it yet," Kim said afterward. "When I stood over that final putt, I knew how important it was. I tried to stay calm and trust my routine. Seeing the ball drop was one of the best moments of my career."
Another standout performer was rookie Mei Lin, who completed the tournament unbeaten. The 25-year-old earned four points from five matches and consistently delivered under pressure. Her convincing victory in the singles session helped establish momentum for Team Asia early in the day.
Captain Park praised Lin's contribution but emphasized the collective effort behind the victory. "Every player contributed something important," she said. "This result belongs to the entire team."
The tournament was not without controversy. During Saturday's four-ball session, a disputed ruling involving a ball near the edge of the cup led to confusion among players and officials. Video clips of the incident quickly circulated online, prompting debate among fans and commentators about whether the correct procedure had been followed.
Several prominent golfers weighed in on social media, with opinions divided over the ruling. Some defended the officials' decision, while others argued that the situation could have been handled differently. The discussion continued long after play had ended, adding another layer of drama to an already memorable tournament.
