Two athletes from South Sudan have arrived safely at the World Athletics Championship host city after an international effort ensured their departure from a region affected by escalating conflict, organizers confirmed on Friday.
Sprinter Daniel Lado and middle-distance runner Mary Ajak left their home country several days earlier and spent time training at a high-performance center in Italy before traveling to the competition venue. Their participation had previously been in doubt after worsening security conditions disrupted transportation and prevented the national team from making final travel arrangements.
The athletes' arrival marks the end of a complex operation involving sports organizations, humanitarian groups, and government agencies. Earlier this month, officials from the South Sudan Athletics Federation had announced that the pair would likely miss the championships because of the unstable situation in the country.
In a statement released by championship organizers, World Athletics President Sebastian Coe praised the coordinated effort that enabled the athletes to attend the event.
"Our primary concern throughout this process has been the safety and well-being of the athletes," Coe said. "Just a short time ago, it appeared impossible for them to travel and compete. Thanks to the cooperation of numerous organizations and governments, they are now here and ready to represent their nation on the world stage."
According to officials, several international partners helped arrange transportation, temporary accommodation, training facilities, and logistical support. Organizers described the operation as an example of how the global sporting community can work together during times of crisis.
The two athletes were not present when national delegations first arrived earlier in the week, prompting concern among supporters and fellow competitors. However, their arrival has been welcomed by athletes from around the world, many of whom expressed support on social media.
For Ajak, the opportunity carries particular significance. She will become one of the few female athletes from her country to compete at the championships in recent years. Scheduled to race in the women's 800 meters, she said she was grateful for the chance to participate despite the difficulties.
"I never stopped hoping that I would make it here," Ajak said. "There were moments when everything seemed uncertain, but I kept training and believing that there would be a way."
Lado is expected to compete in the men's 400-meter event later next week. He said the experience of reaching the championships after such challenges has already become one of the most meaningful moments of his career.
Championship officials stated that support services will remain available to both athletes throughout the event and afterward if needed. They emphasized that ensuring the competitors' welfare remains a top priority as they prepare to take part in their respective events.

(CNN) Professional tennis players are receiving a competitive share of the sport's growing revenues, according to one of the game's most influential administrators, Mark Reynolds. Reynolds spoke after the International Tennis Federation unveiled a major long-term partnership with a global technology company, a deal that he described as a transformative step for the sport.
Beginning next season, the agreement will bring substantial investment into several levels of professional tennis. According to the federation, total prize money across its premier tournaments will surpass $300 million for the first time, while a number of lower-tier events will also see guaranteed increases in funding.
The announcement arrives at a time when questions are being raised about the future structure of professional tennis. In recent months, reports have emerged about a proposed independent circuit backed by private investors that would feature many of the world's top-ranked players competing in a limited number of high-profile events.
Supporters of the concept argue that it could generate greater earnings and more frequent matchups between elite players. Several athletes have acknowledged the appeal of seeing the sport's biggest stars face one another more often throughout the season.
One player who addressed the issue recently was Grand Slam champion Alex Moreno. Speaking ahead of a tournament in Mexico, Moreno suggested that fans would likely welcome more encounters between leading competitors.
"The public wants to see the best players competing against each other as often as possible," Moreno said. "The challenge is finding the right balance between creating those opportunities and preserving the flexibility players currently have."
Unlike many major North American sports leagues, professional tennis does not operate under a traditional team-based revenue-sharing structure. Nevertheless, prize funds, sponsorship opportunities, and performance bonuses have grown significantly in recent years.
Reynolds believes comparisons with leagues such as the NBA or NFL can be misleading. While acknowledging that the systems differ considerably, he argued that tennis players are benefiting from the sport's commercial success.
"Our athletes are compensated at a very high level," Reynolds said. "People often compare tennis directly to team sports, but the business models are fundamentally different. When you look at the total opportunities available to players, I believe the current system remains highly competitive."
According to Reynolds, the newly announced partnership was the result of negotiations that had been underway for several years and was not influenced by discussions surrounding alternative tours.
Beyond the headline increase in prize money, he emphasized that the investment would support player development programs, coaching resources, sports science initiatives, and athlete welfare services.
The federation also plans to expand support for junior competitors and athletes transitioning from developmental circuits to the professional ranks. Additional resources will be directed toward injury prevention, nutrition programs, and mental health services.
Reynolds added that inclusivity would remain a central focus. Funding has been allocated to wheelchair tennis, community outreach projects, and grassroots programs designed to make the sport accessible to a wider range of participants.
"This is about much more than rewarding the top players," he said. "We want to strengthen every level of the game and ensure that future generations have more opportunities to succeed."

(CNN) Andy Murray found himself in a lighthearted exchange with the chair umpire during Tuesday's first-round match at the Geneva Open after a sudden rain shower interrupted his service motion.
As Murray prepared to serve at a crucial stage of the second set, a brief burst of rain began falling across the court. The former world No. 1 stepped away from the baseline and waited for conditions to improve, prompting the umpire to warn him about exceeding the allotted time between points.
"You want me to serve in that?" Murray asked while gesturing toward the sky. "I can control where I hit the ball, but I can't control when the clouds decide to open up."
The three-time Grand Slam champion smiled as the crowd reacted to the exchange, but the warning stood. Play continued shortly afterward once the rain eased.
Murray ultimately fell 6-4, 3-6, 6-3 to Spain's Roberto Carvajal, a lower-ranked opponent who capitalized on a strong final set to secure the upset victory.
The defeat continues a difficult run of results for Murray as he attempts to build momentum ahead of the clay-court season's biggest events. Injuries and inconsistent form have limited his progress this year, and he has struggled to put together consecutive wins on tour.
For Carvajal, the victory marked another impressive result against a former major champion. The Spaniard has now won three of his last four matches against players who have previously been ranked inside the world's top 10.

(CNN) Fueled by an energetic home crowd and a series of dominant performances, Team International stormed to a 7-3 lead over Team Europe after the opening day of the Continental Cup in Melbourne, Australia.
The hosts set the tone early during the morning session, winning three of the four opening matches to establish control of the competition. While Europe's top pairing of Viktor Hansen and Marco Silva managed to secure an important point, the International side consistently outperformed its opponents in key moments.
Rookies Ethan Cole and Liam Brooks delivered one of the standout performances of the day, cruising to a convincing 6&5 victory over experienced European duo Daniel Hart and Oliver Jensen. Elsewhere, veterans Ryan Carter and Ben Matthews edged a tightly contested match, while world No. 4 Lucas Grant teamed up with Michael Reed to earn another point for the hosts.
The afternoon session brought little relief for Europe. Grant and Brooks continued their impressive form with a narrow victory, and the pairing of Carter and Jason Walker overwhelmed their opponents to extend the lead further.
One of the day's most dramatic contests featured European stars Hansen and Stefan Berg against Cole and Matthews. The lead changed hands several times before Berg holed a lengthy birdie putt on the final hole, allowing Europe to salvage a valuable half-point.
The final match of the session also went down to the wire. European pair Silva and Jensen appeared on course for victory after building an early advantage, but the International team fought back over the closing stretch. Neither side could gain a decisive edge, and the match ended all square.
Excitement had been building around the venue from the early hours of the morning. Thousands of spectators packed the grandstands well before the first groups teed off, creating a lively atmosphere throughout the course.
Calm weather conditions during the opening session encouraged aggressive play, and scoring opportunities were plentiful. Several matches remained close through the opening holes, but the International team gradually separated itself with a combination of accurate approach shots and clutch putting.
Cole and Brooks were particularly impressive. The young pair birdied four of their first six holes and never allowed their opponents back into the contest. Their dominant display quickly became one of the major talking points of the day.
Conditions became more challenging in the afternoon as strong coastal winds swept across the course. Players were forced to adjust their club selections and shot strategies, but the home side appeared more comfortable dealing with the changing conditions.
Grant and Brooks adapted quickly, building an early lead that they protected through the closing holes. Carter and Walker were equally effective, producing a steady stream of birdies while avoiding costly mistakes.
Despite trailing by four points, Team Europe remained optimistic after play concluded. Captain Henrik Olsen praised his players' determination and insisted that the competition remained far from over.
"There is still a long way to go," Olsen told reporters. "We've been in difficult situations before. We need to play better tomorrow, but everyone still believes we can turn this around."

Editor's Note: This article is part of an ongoing series examining the experiences of women in public and religious spaces around the world.
(CNN) Several women who attended a major religious gathering in South Asia have told CNN they experienced incidents of sexual harassment during the event, describing encounters that ranged from unwanted touching to verbal abuse in densely crowded areas.
The women said they decided to speak publicly after a social media post describing a similar experience gained widespread attention online earlier this year. The post prompted dozens of responses from women who said they had faced comparable situations during large-scale religious events.
The gathering attracts millions of worshipers annually and is considered one of the most significant spiritual occasions for participants. Organizers face major logistical challenges due to the size of the crowds, particularly in areas where pilgrims move through narrow routes or gather at key ceremonial locations.
Several women told CNN that the incidents occurred in crowded sections where movement was restricted and individuals were often pressed closely together. Some said they reported their experiences to security personnel, while others chose not to out of fear, embarrassment, or uncertainty about how their complaints would be received.
Event authorities said in a statement that harassment of any kind is unacceptable and that reports of misconduct are taken seriously. Officials added that additional security personnel, including more female officers, have been deployed in recent years to improve safety and encourage reporting.
The following accounts have been edited for clarity and length. Some interviewees requested anonymity because of concerns about public reaction.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
I attended the gathering in 2018 with several relatives. During one of the busiest periods, I became separated from my family for a short time. While moving through a crowded passageway, I felt someone repeatedly touching my back and waist.
At first, I assumed it was accidental because there were so many people around me. But it continued even after I changed direction. When I turned around, the man moved away quickly and disappeared into the crowd.
I remember feeling shocked more than anything else. I had traveled there for spiritual reasons, and the last thing I expected was to be worried about my personal safety.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience happened in broad daylight. I was walking with friends toward a prayer area when a man began making comments about my appearance. We ignored him and continued walking, but he followed us for several minutes.
Later that day, in a more crowded area, someone grabbed my arm from behind. I pulled away immediately, but the incident left me feeling anxious for the rest of the trip.
I never reported it because I assumed nothing would come of it. Looking back, I wish I had at least tried.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was 19 years old when I attended the event for the first time. The crowds were enormous, and there were moments when it was difficult to move freely.
During one procession, a man standing behind me repeatedly pushed against me despite having plenty of space to step back. I felt uncomfortable and tried to move away, but there was little room.
For a long time, I convinced myself that I might have misunderstood what happened. Reading other women's stories later made me realize that many people had experienced similar situations.
Sara Ahmed, living in Canada
I attended with my parents several years ago. One incident happened near a marketplace where pilgrims often gathered after prayers.
A man approached and began asking personal questions. When I ignored him, he continued following me for several minutes. Eventually, I joined a larger group of women and he left.
Nothing physical happened, but the experience stayed with me because I felt vulnerable in a place where I expected to feel secure.
Many people assume these things cannot happen in religious settings, but large crowds can create opportunities for misconduct if safeguards are not strong enough.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I have attended the gathering twice. During my first visit, I experienced unwanted touching in a crowded section near one of the main ritual sites.
I did not tell anyone at the time. I was young, embarrassed, and worried that people would question my actions rather than the behavior of the person responsible.
Years later, I shared the story with friends and discovered that some of them had experienced similar incidents. That was the first time I realized how common these conversations were becoming.

The following testimonies have been condensed and edited for clarity. Certain names have been withheld at the request of the interviewees.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
I was attending with family members in 2018. At one point, we became separated in a very crowded section. While trying to make my way through, I noticed someone repeatedly touching my side and lower back.
Initially, I dismissed it as accidental contact. There were people everywhere. But when it kept happening even after I changed position, it became clear it wasn't random.
The person disappeared into the crowd before I could react. What stayed with me afterward was the surprise. I had never imagined I would encounter something like that during a religious journey.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience started with comments. A man began speaking to me and a group of friends as we walked toward a prayer area. We ignored him, but he continued following us for a while.
Later, someone grabbed my arm in a crowded section of the site. It happened quickly, and I pulled away immediately.
I didn't report either incident. At the time, I felt it would be pointless, although now I'm not so sure.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was attending for the first time when I was 19. The scale of the crowd was overwhelming, especially during one of the processions.
A man behind me kept pressing forward despite there being enough space not to. I remember feeling trapped because moving away wasn't easy.
For years I questioned my own interpretation of the situation. Only after reading other women's accounts did I realize my experience wasn't unique.
Sara Ahmed, Canada
One of my most uncomfortable moments happened near a busy market area after prayers.
A stranger started asking personal questions and continued following me when I chose not to answer. Eventually I moved closer to a larger group of women, and he stopped.
Compared with some stories, it may seem minor. Even so, it changed how I felt for the remainder of the trip. I became much more cautious about where I went and who was around me.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I experienced unwanted touching during my first visit to the pilgrimage site. It happened in a densely packed area where people were moving slowly.
I never mentioned it to anyone. I was embarrassed and worried that the conversation would somehow focus on my behavior rather than the actions of the person involved.
Years later, I finally talked about it with friends. To my surprise, several had stories of their own. That realization made me understand why more women are choosing to speak publicly about these experiences today.

(CNN) After more than a decade in power, Prime Minister Sofia Lindberg is preparing to leave office, marking the end of an era that has shaped politics in Sweden and across Northern Europe.
For years, political observers assumed that Lindberg's center-right Alliance Party would remain the dominant force even after her departure. However, recent opinion polls have complicated that assumption. A survey published last week placed the Progressive Green Movement ahead of the Alliance for the first time in nearly a decade, triggering intense debate about the country's political future.
While Alliance officials argued that the result reflected a temporary surge following the Greens' selection of a new leader, analysts say the broader trend cannot be ignored. Polling throughout the year has suggested growing support for environmental and social reform policies, particularly among younger and urban voters.
Even if the Greens fail to win the largest share of the vote, many experts believe they are now positioned to play a central role in coalition negotiations after next year's election.
"The question is no longer whether the Greens will be part of government," said Helena Markström, a political scientist at Stockholm University. "The question is how much influence they will have over the agenda."
Despite their rise, few expect a dramatic shift in Sweden's foreign policy. Over recent years, mainstream parties have increasingly adopted positions on climate policy, renewable energy, and sustainable development that were once associated almost exclusively with the Greens.
The implications could nevertheless extend beyond Sweden. As one of the European Union's more influential member states, Sweden often plays an important role in debates over environmental regulation, trade policy, and digital governance.
A Green-led government would also symbolize a broader transformation taking place across Europe, where traditional center-right and center-left parties have struggled to maintain their historic dominance.
Many political strategists argue that established parties are facing pressure from multiple directions. Progressive movements are attracting younger voters concerned about climate change and inequality, while nationalist and anti-establishment groups continue to draw support from voters frustrated with political institutions.
"The old political coalitions are becoming harder to sustain," said one European diplomat. "Voters are increasingly looking for alternatives, whether on the left, the right, or outside the traditional system altogether."
That dynamic is creating uncertainty in Brussels, where policymakers are already preparing for a series of elections across the continent. Several European governments face growing pressure from emerging parties that challenge long-standing assumptions about integration and economic policy.
The situation is particularly significant because neighboring countries are undergoing political shifts of their own. In Finland and Denmark, newer parties have gained influence, while debates over immigration, energy policy, and economic competitiveness continue to reshape electoral landscapes.
A more environmentally focused government in Sweden could strengthen efforts to accelerate the EU's climate agenda. However, differences with other member states could complicate negotiations on industrial policy, energy security, and trade.
At the same time, some analysts caution against overstating the likelihood of radical change. Coalition politics often require compromise, and even a strong Green showing would almost certainly require cooperation with other parties.
Still, the broader message is clear. Political loyalties that once seemed stable are becoming more fluid across Europe. Whether the next government is led by the Alliance Party or the Greens, the election will serve as another test of how traditional political institutions adapt to an increasingly fragmented electorate.
"The era of predictable politics is fading," Markström said. "The parties that succeed will be the ones that recognize how quickly voters' priorities are evolving."

(CNN) It was a night of celebration and dominance for the Denver Nuggets as they officially began their title defense in front of a packed home crowd.
Before tipoff of the 2024-25 NBA season, the franchise honored its championship-winning team with a banner-raising ceremony and the presentation of championship rings. Players, coaches, and staff gathered at center court as highlights from the historic season played on the arena's video boards, drawing loud cheers from fans.
The championship rings were designed to reflect key moments from the title-winning campaign. Team officials said the rings feature hundreds of diamonds symbolizing victories accumulated throughout the season, while custom details commemorate the franchise's first NBA championship and its playoff run.
Several elements were personalized for each player, including engraved statistics and references to memorable postseason performances. The organization described the rings as a tribute to both the team's success and the city's support during the championship journey.
The festivities continued once the game began.
Led by reigning Finals MVP Nikola Jokić, the Nuggets defeated the Dallas Mavericks 118-101 in an impressive opening-night performance. Jokić recorded 29 points, 13 rebounds, and 11 assists, securing a triple-double as Denver controlled much of the contest.
Dallas remained competitive through the first half behind Luka Dončić's scoring, but the Nuggets gradually pulled away in the third quarter with a series of defensive stops and efficient offensive possessions.
After the victory, Jokić acknowledged the significance of the ceremony but emphasized that the team was already focused on the future.
"The banner is something that will always be there," he said. "We accomplished something special, but a new season starts now. We have to keep improving and keep working if we want another opportunity."
Meanwhile, another championship contender experienced a disappointing start to its campaign.
The Phoenix Suns opened their season with a 115-108 home loss to the Minnesota Timberwolves. Despite strong performances from Devin Booker and Kevin Durant, the Suns struggled to contain Minnesota's balanced attack during the second half.
Booker finished with 31 points, while Durant added 28. However, Phoenix received limited offensive production from the rest of its lineup as Minnesota steadily built momentum after halftime.
Anthony Edwards led the Timberwolves with 27 points and provided several key plays late in the game to secure the victory.
Afterward, Edwards praised his team's resilience but said there was still significant room for improvement.
"We didn't play a perfect game," he told reporters. "There are a lot of things we can clean up. But winning on opening night against a team like that gives us confidence."
Minnesota's victory highlighted one of the team's strengths: depth. Contributions came from throughout the roster, allowing the Timberwolves to withstand scoring runs and maintain control in crucial moments.
For Denver, however, the evening belonged to celebration. A championship banner now hangs permanently above the court, and the team's successful start offered an encouraging reminder of why many expect the Nuggets to remain among the league's top contenders this season.

(CNN) National team defender Alex Morgan has announced that he has been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will temporarily step away from professional soccer while undergoing treatment.
The 26-year-old, who plays for Championship club Bristol City, shared the news in a statement released on social media Thursday, explaining that doctors detected the condition during a series of medical examinations earlier this month.
"I have recently been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will begin treatment in the coming days," Morgan wrote.
According to health authorities, testicular cancer is one of the most treatable forms of cancer when detected early. Treatment options vary depending on the stage and type of the disease but often have high success rates.
Morgan said the diagnosis had come as an unexpected shock to both him and his family but emphasized that doctors were optimistic about his recovery.
"Naturally, receiving this news has been difficult, but I have been reassured by the specialists overseeing my care that the outlook is very positive," he said. "My focus now is on completing treatment, recovering fully, and returning to football when the time is right."
The defender had recently withdrawn from international duty after reporting persistent discomfort that required further medical assessment.
In his statement, Morgan thanked the medical staff at both club and national team level for their support and quick action.
"I am incredibly grateful to the doctors, nurses, and specialists who have guided me through this process so far," he said. "I also want to thank the medical teams at Bristol City and the national federation for acting quickly and ensuring I received the tests I needed."
Morgan has been a regular presence for club and country over the past several seasons and is regarded as one of the brightest defensive talents in the national setup.
Following the announcement, messages of support poured in from teammates, clubs, coaches, and supporters across the football community.
Bristol City released a statement expressing its full backing for the player during his recovery.
"Alex's health and well-being are our only priorities at this time," the club said. "He will receive every possible support from the club, and there will be no pressure regarding his return to football."
Club officials added that Morgan would be given as much time as necessary to focus on treatment and rehabilitation.
The player also asked for privacy while he undergoes medical care.
"I appreciate the kindness and encouragement people have already shown," he wrote. "At the same time, I hope everyone will respect the privacy of myself and my family as we navigate the months ahead."
He concluded by thanking supporters for their messages and expressing confidence that he would return to the sport in the future.
"I look forward to being back on the pitch and doing what I love again. Until then, my attention is fully on recovery."

(CNN) College volleyball player Emma Richardson was preparing for an away match with her team when she received news that severe flooding had swept through her hometown in eastern Tennessee.
"I couldn't believe what I was seeing," Richardson told CNN Sports as she described viewing photos and videos of the destruction on her phone. "You recognize the streets and buildings, but everything looks completely different."
The flooding followed days of heavy rainfall and left large sections of the community underwater. Homes, businesses, and public facilities suffered extensive damage, forcing many residents to evacuate.
For Richardson, the disaster felt deeply personal.
"This is the place where I grew up," she said. "My family is here, my friends are here, and so many of the people who helped me along the way still live in town. Seeing it happen was heartbreaking."
After competing in the match, the senior athlete decided she wanted to contribute to recovery efforts in any way she could.
Within days, Richardson launched an online fundraising campaign to support families affected by the flooding. She shared the initiative through social media and encouraged fellow athletes, alumni, and supporters to spread the word.
University volleyball player Emma Richardson created a fundraiser for flood victims in her hometown.
Richardson said she was inspired by athletes who have used their public profiles to support communities during times of crisis.
"You see people step up when disaster strikes, and it reminds you that even small actions can make a difference," she said. "I figured if I could help raise awareness and bring in some donations, it was worth trying."
The response exceeded her expectations.
By the end of the week, the fundraiser had collected more than $120,000 from thousands of donors across the country. The proceeds are expected to support local relief organizations, emergency assistance programs, and rebuilding efforts.
Earlier this week, Richardson returned home to volunteer alongside residents and local organizations. She spent several days helping remove debris, distributing supplies, and assisting families as they began the long recovery process.
"It means a lot to be here in person," she said. "Donating money is important, but being able to work alongside people and listen to what they're going through makes it feel more real."
The experience reinforced what she believes is one of her hometown's defining qualities.
"People here look after each other," Richardson said. "Even after everything that's happened, you can see neighbors helping neighbors everywhere you go."
She added that giving back to the community that supported her athletic journey was something she felt compelled to do.
"This town has given me so much over the years. Being able to help, even in a small way, feels like the least I can do."

(CNN) For many members of Canada's Indigenous communities, the past few years have brought a mix of recognition, frustration, and renewed demands for change.
The discovery of unmarked graves at former residential school sites reignited a national conversation about historical injustices and the ongoing effects of policies that separated Indigenous children from their families. While governments and institutions pledged action, many Indigenous leaders say meaningful progress has been uneven.
"It brought a lot of emotions back to the surface," said community advocate Rachel Whitecloud. "Many people felt grief, but also a sense that stories our families had been telling for generations were finally being heard."
Whitecloud, who works with youth programs in Manitoba, said the public response initially gave many people hope that long-standing issues would receive greater attention.
"There was a feeling that maybe things would be different this time," she said. "That people would listen, learn, and take responsibility."
Yet that optimism has often been tempered by disappointment. Indigenous activists argue that public expressions of support have not always translated into concrete improvements in areas such as housing, healthcare, education, and access to clean drinking water.
For many, media coverage has become part of the debate itself. Some advocates say news organizations have increased attention to Indigenous issues, while others argue that coverage can still be inconsistent or overly focused on moments of crisis.
"There are times when our communities are visible only when something tragic happens," said university lecturer Daniel Cardinal. "The everyday realities, the successes, the resilience—those stories don't always receive the same attention."
The discussion has unfolded alongside broader conversations about inequality and representation taking place across Canada. Public institutions have faced growing pressure to address historical grievances and improve relationships with Indigenous peoples.
Younger Indigenous creators have increasingly turned to social media and independent platforms to tell their own stories. Among them is content producer Maya Stone, whose online videos discussing Indigenous identity, culture, and politics attract hundreds of thousands of views each month.
"For a long time, other people were speaking about us," Stone said. "Now more Indigenous people are speaking for themselves and building their own audiences."
She believes digital platforms have helped create space for conversations that were previously overlooked by mainstream outlets.
At the same time, many activists caution that awareness alone is not enough. Symbolic gestures, they argue, must be followed by lasting reforms.
"There is more public understanding than there used to be," Whitecloud said. "But understanding is only the beginning. The real question is what happens next."
Government officials have acknowledged that significant challenges remain and have pointed to ongoing initiatives aimed at improving infrastructure, expanding services, and supporting Indigenous-led programs. Critics, however, say progress has been too slow.
Despite differing views on the pace of change, there is broad agreement that the national conversation has shifted. Issues that once received limited public attention are now being discussed more openly across the country.
Whether that leads to lasting transformation remains uncertain. But many Indigenous Canadians say the expectation for accountability is stronger than it was a decade ago.
"The conversation isn't going away," Cardinal said. "People have waited too long for that."

A version of this story appeared in the September 12 edition of CNN's Royal News, a weekly newsletter covering developments involving the British royal family, public engagements, and events behind palace walls.
London (CNN) When photographs emerged showing Prince George accompanying his parents at a series of official engagements over the summer, royal observers quickly began debating what role the young prince might play in the future of the monarchy.
The discussion reflects a broader fascination with the royal line of succession and how the institution prepares younger generations for public life. As the eldest child of Prince William and Catherine, Princess of Wales, George remains second in line to the throne behind his father.
While public appearances by senior royals are often carefully planned, constitutional experts point out that succession itself is governed by established law. Individual family members do not choose their place in the order, nor can it be altered by personal preference.
A file photograph shows Prince William and Prince George attending a public event together.
The structure has remained largely unchanged for generations, although Parliament has occasionally updated the rules. In 2013, changes ended the system under which younger sons could take precedence over older daughters in the line of succession.
As a result, Princess Charlotte retained her position ahead of her younger brother, Prince Louis, marking a significant modernization of succession rules.
For many royal watchers, however, legal details are less interesting than questions about how younger royals will adapt to increasing public attention.
The monarchy has faced considerable scrutiny in recent years, with debates surrounding its future, relevance, and relationship with the public becoming more frequent.
Supporters argue that continuity remains one of the institution's greatest strengths. Critics maintain that hereditary systems are difficult to reconcile with modern democratic values.
Regardless of those debates, George's future role remains largely defined by circumstances beyond his control. His position within the royal hierarchy was determined at birth and would only change through events affecting those ahead of him.
WHAT ELSE IS HAPPENING?
King attends charity awards ceremony
The King traveled to Manchester this week to present awards recognizing community volunteers from across the United Kingdom. During the ceremony, he praised individuals who had contributed to local initiatives supporting education, health services, and environmental projects.
Speaking to attendees, he emphasized the importance of civic engagement and thanked volunteers for their dedication to helping others.
Prince and Princess of Wales visit youth center
The Prince and Princess of Wales spent part of the week visiting a youth center in Birmingham focused on mentoring and career development programs.
The visit included discussions with students, teachers, and local organizers. Kensington Palace said the engagement formed part of the couple's ongoing interest in education and early intervention initiatives.
Royal documentary attracts attention
A new documentary examining the history of royal residences premiered this week on British television.
The program explores the development of several royal estates and includes interviews with historians, architects, and former palace staff. Early reviews have been mixed, though audience interest appears strong.
PHOTO OF THE WEEK
King Charles speaks with conservation volunteers during a visit to a coastal restoration project.
The King joined environmental groups working to restore natural habitats along part of Britain's coastline. Organizers said the project aims to improve biodiversity while protecting vulnerable ecosystems from erosion and climate-related challenges.
During the visit, Charles spoke with volunteers and researchers involved in the effort and reiterated his long-standing interest in environmental issues.
FROM THE ROYAL VAULT
This week marks the anniversary of the opening of Buckingham Palace to summer visitors in the 1990s.
The decision represented a significant change in how the royal family interacted with the public, allowing visitors access to parts of the palace previously seen only through official photographs and television broadcasts.
Over the years, the annual opening has become one of London's most popular attractions, offering visitors a glimpse into one of the country's most recognizable institutions.

A crowdfunding platform that promotes itself as a champion of free expression has become a fundraising hub for a range of controversial political figures and activist groups, drawing criticism from watchdog organizations and renewed scrutiny from payment providers.
An examination of campaigns hosted on the platform found dozens of fundraisers connected to politically charged causes, including legal defense efforts, travel expenses for demonstrations, and investigations into disputed election claims. Several campaigns have collectively raised hundreds of thousands of dollars from supporters across the country.
Among the beneficiaries are activists facing criminal charges, organizers of large-scale political rallies, and media personalities who claim they have been unfairly targeted by mainstream institutions. Supporters argue that these individuals deserve access to financial assistance regardless of their political views, while critics contend that such fundraising can strengthen extremist networks and enable harmful activities.
The platform's leadership maintains that its role is not to judge the popularity of a cause but to determine whether it complies with the law and the company's policies. According to company executives, campaigns are generally permitted as long as they do not explicitly advocate violence, discrimination, or illegal conduct.
"We believe people should have the opportunity to support causes they care about," one executive said in a recent interview. "Our responsibility is to provide a service, not to act as an ideological gatekeeper."
That position has sparked debate among advocacy groups that monitor online extremism. Some researchers argue that fundraising infrastructure is a critical component of political movements, particularly those operating on the fringes of public discourse. Restricting access to financial resources, they say, can reduce the reach and influence of organizations associated with violence or hate.
Others view the issue through the lens of free expression. Legal scholars note that raising money for legal representation, public advocacy, or political organizing is often protected under constitutional principles, even when the underlying cause is controversial.
The company has also faced pressure from financial partners. Several payment processors have reportedly suspended services for certain campaigns or declined to handle transactions associated with higher-risk activities. In some cases, fundraising pages remain visible online even though donations can no longer be accepted.
Despite the controversy, the platform continues to grow. Originally established to support charitable and community-based projects, it now hosts campaigns ranging from medical expenses and disaster relief to political activism and legal defense funds. Company leaders say increasing traffic and donations have significantly expanded the platform's reach in recent years.
The debate surrounding the site reflects a broader question facing the technology industry: should digital platforms remain neutral hosts for lawful activity, or should they take a more active role in determining which causes deserve access to their services? As political polarization continues to shape public life, that question is unlikely to disappear anytime soon.

A crowdfunding website that presents itself as a defender of open participation has found itself at the center of another debate over online fundraising and political activism.
A review of campaigns on the platform shows that a number of politically contentious figures and groups have used it to collect money for legal bills, travel costs, public events, and other projects. Some of those campaigns have attracted substantial support, bringing in tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars.
Supporters of the platform argue that access to fundraising tools should not depend on whether a person's views are widely accepted. Many of the campaigns involve activists, commentators, or organizers who say they have been marginalized by larger institutions or removed from other online services.
Critics see the situation differently. Organizations that track extremist activity argue that money is often what allows controversial movements to expand, organize events, and maintain a public presence. In their view, platforms that provide fundraising opportunities cannot completely separate themselves from the causes they host.
Company representatives reject that criticism. They say their decisions are based primarily on legality and compliance with site policies, not on political ideology. If a campaign does not promote unlawful behavior or explicitly target people with hateful content, it is generally allowed to remain online.
The company's approach has not insulated it from outside pressure. Payment providers and financial partners have occasionally restricted transactions linked to certain campaigns, leaving some fundraising pages active but unable to receive donations.
The controversy highlights a larger disagreement that extends well beyond a single website. Technology companies increasingly face demands to decide where the line should be drawn between open access and social responsibility. Some believe platforms should remove controversial causes before they gain momentum. Others worry that giving private companies that power could create a different set of problems.
Meanwhile, the platform continues to expand beyond politics. Alongside the headline-grabbing campaigns are far more ordinary fundraising efforts: families covering medical expenses, local communities supporting disaster victims, and individuals seeking help during difficult times.
As debates over speech, accountability, and online platforms continue, services like this one are likely to remain under close public scrutiny. Whether they are viewed as neutral tools or active participants in political life may depend largely on who is looking.

(CNN) The college football postseason field is officially set, with the selection committee announcing the four teams that will compete for the national championship on Sunday.
The No. 1-ranked Texas Longhorns (12-1), No. 2 Oregon Ducks (12-1), No. 3 Florida State Seminoles (13-0) and No. 4 Washington Huskies (12-1) secured spots in this year's College Football Playoff.
The semifinal matchups, scheduled for New Year's Day, will feature top-seeded Texas facing fourth-seeded Washington in the Sugar Bowl at Caesars Superdome in New Orleans, Louisiana. Meanwhile, Oregon will take on Florida State in the Rose Bowl at the Rose Bowl Stadium in Pasadena, California.
The winners of those contests will advance to the national championship game, which is set to be played later in January at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas.
Florida State's inclusion marks a significant achievement for the program, which completed an undefeated regular season and captured its conference championship. The Seminoles relied on a balanced roster and strong defensive performances throughout the year to remain among the nation's elite teams.
Texas earned the top seed after winning its conference title game and recording several victories over ranked opponents. Under head coach Steve Sarkisian, the Longhorns have returned to national prominence following several seasons outside the championship conversation.
Oregon enters the playoff after a dominant campaign that featured one of the country's highest-scoring offenses. The Ducks secured key victories during conference play and finished the season with momentum following a convincing championship-game performance.
Washington claimed the final playoff position after compiling a strong résumé highlighted by multiple wins against ranked competition. The Huskies' passing attack played a central role in their success throughout the season.
Just outside the playoff field, the fifth-ranked Penn State Nittany Lions (11-2) narrowly missed selection despite another successful year. The team is expected to receive an invitation to a major bowl game and finish the season against a highly ranked opponent.
The sixth-ranked LSU Tigers (10-3) also remained in contention until the final rankings were released. LSU's explosive offense helped keep the Tigers in the national discussion throughout much of the season.
With the playoff bracket now finalized, attention turns to the semifinal games, where four of the country's top programs will compete for the opportunity to play for a national championship.

At the Presidents Cup on Saturday, veteran golfer Justin Thomas added another milestone to his already impressive international career, becoming one of the most successful American players in team competition history.
The 32-year-old secured his 18th match victory in the event after teaming up with Patrick Cantlay to defeat the International Team pairing of Sungjae Im and Corey Conners in the morning foursomes session. The American duo rallied after a difficult start, overturning a two-hole deficit midway through the round to earn a 2&1 victory.
It marked their second consecutive win together during the tournament, having also claimed a point in Friday's team matches. Thomas has become known for his strong performances in team events and has consistently delivered points for the United States throughout his appearances.
Since making his debut several years ago, Thomas has established himself as one of the team's most reliable competitors. Entering this year's competition, he was already among the leading active American players in total points earned.
The latest victory moved him further up the all-time standings and reinforced his reputation as a player who thrives under pressure. His partnership with Cantlay has been particularly effective, with the pair demonstrating strong chemistry throughout the week.
Despite reaching another personal milestone, Thomas focused on the overall contest rather than his individual achievement. Speaking after the round, he emphasized that the team's position in the competition remained the priority.
"It's nice, of course, but we're here to win as a team," Thomas told reporters. "Personal records are something you can appreciate later. Right now, we're trying to keep momentum on our side and continue putting points on the board."
He added that the International Team had been highly competitive and that the United States could not afford to become complacent.
"There are still a lot of matches left," he said. "We've played some good golf, but every session matters. We need to stay focused and keep fighting for every point."

(CNN) The death of a woman whose pregnancy was complicated by severe fetal abnormalities has intensified debate over abortion laws in a European country where access to the procedure has become increasingly restricted.
Thousands of demonstrators gathered in major cities over the weekend after activists linked the woman's death to legislation that sharply limits the circumstances under which pregnancies can be terminated. Protesters argued that legal uncertainty surrounding abortion has made some physicians reluctant to intervene even when a patient's health is deteriorating.
The woman, who was in the second trimester of pregnancy, was admitted to a regional hospital after experiencing serious medical complications, according to statements from her family and legal representatives. Prenatal examinations had reportedly identified significant abnormalities in the fetus.
Family members said doctors chose to continue monitoring the pregnancy rather than perform an immediate termination. As the woman's condition worsened, she developed a severe infection and later died despite medical efforts to save her.
Her death has become a focal point for abortion-rights advocates, who say restrictive laws have created confusion among healthcare providers about when intervention is legally permitted. Demonstrators carried signs and candles during rallies held in several cities, calling for reforms that would provide doctors with greater discretion in emergency situations.
Lawyers representing the family have requested a formal investigation into the medical decisions made during the woman's treatment. They argue that both individual clinical judgments and the broader legal environment should be examined.
Hospital officials expressed condolences to the family and said all actions taken by medical staff were consistent with existing regulations and professional standards. Authorities have launched a review to determine whether any procedural errors occurred.
The case quickly gained national attention after details were shared on social media. A slogan translating to "Never Again" spread widely online and became a rallying cry for demonstrators demanding changes to the country's abortion policies.
Government leaders have rejected suggestions that abortion restrictions directly caused the woman's death. Officials maintain that current laws allow physicians to act when a patient's life or health is in danger and argue that any failures were related to medical decision-making rather than legislation itself.
Supporters of the restrictions contend that the laws are designed to protect unborn children and should not be blamed before investigations are complete. Several lawmakers urged the public to wait for official findings before drawing conclusions about responsibility.
Medical and legal experts remain divided. Some argue that physicians may become overly cautious when legal penalties are unclear, while others insist that existing exceptions provide sufficient flexibility for doctors to respond to emergencies.
The case has drawn comparisons to incidents in other countries where the deaths of pregnant women sparked national conversations about reproductive rights and ultimately influenced legislative change.
For activists seeking reform, the woman's death represents what they describe as the human cost of restrictive policies. They hope the public attention generated by the case will lead lawmakers to revisit the country's abortion laws and clarify the circumstances under which medical intervention can occur.

Six years after his most recent major triumph, Rory McIlroy has put himself back in contention for another memorable victory. The four-time major champion surged into the lead on Friday during the second round of the US Open, finishing with a three-shot advantage over the rest of the field.
The Northern Irishman looked in complete control for much of the day. He collected five birdies across his first 13 holes and appeared on course for an even lower score before a pair of late bogeys reduced his advantage. Nevertheless, he ended the round at 6-under par, putting himself in a strong position heading into the weekend.
Despite the disappointing finish, McIlroy remained upbeat afterward and said he was pleased with the overall quality of his play. During his post-round interview, he was informed that several rivals had dropped shots on the closing holes, leaving him alone at the top of the leaderboard.
"That's nice to hear, but there are still two long days ahead," McIlroy said. "I've put myself in a good position, but nothing has been won yet. The course is demanding, and there are a lot of great players chasing."
If McIlroy can maintain his lead through Sunday, it would mark his first major championship victory in more than a decade. Since lifting the PGA Championship trophy in 2014, he has come close on several occasions but has been unable to add to his major tally.
After recent tournaments in which inconsistency affected his results, McIlroy credited his improved performance to better mental preparation. He explained that he has spent considerable time working on concentration and staying committed to every shot.
"I've been focusing on keeping my attention in the present moment," he said. "It's easy to think ahead in a tournament, but I've tried to stay disciplined and approach each shot with the same level of commitment."
He added that mental training has become increasingly important as his career has progressed.
"You have to keep developing every part of your game," McIlroy said. "For me, that includes focus, patience, and trusting what I see. Those things can make a huge difference over four rounds."

He suggested that recent improvements have less to do with swing changes and more to do with what is happening between his ears. In recent weeks, he has devoted extra attention to maintaining concentration for long periods and avoiding the lapses that can prove costly in major championships.

McIlroy believes that aspect of the game becomes even more important with experience. Physical skills still matter, but so does the ability to remain patient and focused when the pressure starts to build.
As he heads into the weekend with the lead, that's the challenge he faces now: not getting ahead of himself and continuing to do the small things well, one shot at a time.

(CNN) A professional basketball coach has been dismissed by his club after making controversial remarks during a post-game interview that drew widespread criticism from fans, advocacy groups and league officials.
The comments were made following his team's narrow loss in a domestic league match on Saturday. Frustrated by what he believed were unfavorable officiating decisions, the coach used an analogy involving domestic violence while discussing the number of fouls awarded to his team.
"We seem to have to go through something extreme before we get a call," the coach said during a television interview. "At this point, it feels like one of our players would have to be assaulted before the referees notice anything."
The remarks quickly sparked backlash on social media, with critics accusing the coach of trivializing a serious issue. Several supporters called on the club to take disciplinary action, while advocacy organizations condemned the language as inappropriate and insensitive.
Later that evening, the club released a statement distancing itself from the comments.
"The views expressed by our head coach do not reflect the values of this organization," the statement said. "We consider the remarks unacceptable and sincerely apologize to anyone offended. The matter is being reviewed internally."
Less than 24 hours later, the team announced that the coach's contract had been terminated with immediate effect.
"Following a review of recent events, the club has decided to part ways with the head coach," the organization said in a statement. "An interim coaching arrangement will be announced in the coming days."
The coach, who enjoyed a lengthy playing career before moving into management, has worked for clubs in several countries and previously led teams to domestic titles. He joined the club before the start of the current season with the aim of rebuilding a struggling roster.
Despite expectations of improvement, results have been disappointing. The team currently sits near the bottom of the league standings after winning only a handful of games through the first half of the campaign.
League officials declined to comment on the dismissal but reiterated their commitment to maintaining professional standards among players, coaches and club personnel.
The incident has renewed discussion about accountability in sports and the consequences public figures may face for comments made during emotionally charged post-match interviews.

(CNN) This year's film awards season has underscored a transformation that has been building for years: streaming platforms are no longer simply competing with traditional movie studios—they are increasingly defining what success looks like in the industry.
Several of the most celebrated films of the year have come not from theatrical releases but from streaming services, whose growing influence has forced award organizations to reconsider how they evaluate cultural impact. As audiences continue shifting their viewing habits toward at-home entertainment, conventional measures of popularity have become less reliable indicators of a film's reach.
For decades, box-office revenue served as the clearest benchmark for determining whether a movie resonated with the public. Today, that metric tells only part of the story. Millions of viewers can watch a film through a subscription service without generating the kind of ticket-sales figures that once dominated industry discussions.
The challenge is that streaming platforms rarely provide detailed audience data comparable to box-office reporting. Nevertheless, certain films have clearly broken through the crowded entertainment landscape, generating extensive online discussion, critical analysis and social-media engagement.
This reality has become increasingly important for award ceremonies seeking to maintain relevance. Recognition often carries greater weight when audiences are familiar with the nominated works. In the streaming era, visibility may come less from theatrical attendance and more from widespread digital consumption.
Traditional media coverage has at times struggled to adapt to these changing dynamics. Discussions about declining movie-ticket sales frequently overlook the possibility that viewers have not abandoned films altogether but have instead changed where and how they watch them.
The shift has also complicated the distinction between prestige cinema and mainstream entertainment. Streaming companies have invested heavily in attracting acclaimed directors, high-profile actors and ambitious projects that can compete for major awards while simultaneously reaching large global audiences.
At the same time, theatrical blockbusters continue to occupy a unique place in the industry. Large-scale franchise films remain among the few releases capable of generating truly massive box-office numbers and shared cultural moments. Their success has prompted recurring debates about whether award organizations should place greater emphasis on films with broad commercial appeal.
In response, some institutions have expanded nominee fields or revised eligibility rules in hopes of creating a balance between artistic recognition and audience engagement. Whether those efforts have succeeded remains a matter of debate.
What seems increasingly clear is that streaming services now occupy a central position in the awards conversation. Films that generate substantial attention online and attract large home-viewing audiences are becoming difficult for voters to ignore, regardless of their theatrical performance.
The long-term consequences of this shift remain uncertain. Audience habits may continue evolving, and the relationship between theaters and streaming platforms is still being negotiated across the industry.
For now, however, recent award outcomes suggest that voters are acknowledging a simple reality: a film's cultural impact can no longer be measured solely by ticket sales. In an entertainment landscape increasingly shaped by digital viewing, success is being defined in new ways.

Streaming companies have adapted aggressively to that reality. They have poured resources into original productions, recruited major filmmakers and built catalogs capable of competing for both awards and audience attention. That contrast has fueled ongoing debate about the role of awards organizations. Some have broadened their nomination fields or adjusted rules in an effort to recognize both artistic achievement and popular appeal.

Hollywood may still be adapting to that reality, but the direction seems clear. The definition of a successful film is evolving, and awards season is increasingly reflecting that transformation.

Bayern Munich's relentless run of form continued on Wednesday as the German champions secured a convincing 3-1 victory over RB Leipzig to advance to the semifinals of the German Cup.
The win extended Bayern's impressive unbeaten streak and kept alive hopes of winning multiple trophies this season. Already leading the Bundesliga standings and still competing in European competition, the team continues to demonstrate the consistency that has made it one of the most feared sides in world football.
Manager Thomas Tuchel praised his players after the match but was careful not to get carried away by discussions of potential silverware. While supporters have begun speculating about another historic campaign, the coach insisted that the focus remains firmly on the next challenge.
"We are playing well, but there is still a long way to go," Tuchel said after the game. "Football changes very quickly, and success depends on maintaining the same level every week."
The victory once again highlighted Bayern's depth. Several key players were rested, yet the team maintained its intensity and control throughout the match. Young talents stepped in alongside experienced internationals, helping Bayern dominate possession and create numerous scoring opportunities.
Much of the club's success this season has been built on a strong defensive foundation. The partnership at the heart of the defense has provided stability, while the midfield has effectively limited opponents' chances before they develop into serious threats.
At the other end of the pitch, Bayern's attacking players continue to deliver. Goals have come from across the squad, reducing the team's dependence on any single individual and making it difficult for opponents to contain them.
With crucial fixtures approaching in both domestic and European competitions, Bayern remains in contention for a remarkable season. The club's supporters are beginning to dream of another trophy-laden campaign, although players and coaches continue to stress the importance of staying focused on one match at a time.
Their next challenge arrives this weekend against a top-four rival, a match that could further strengthen Bayern's position and bring them another step closer to achieving their ambitions.

The result sends Bayern into the semifinals of the German Cup and adds to a growing list of positive results. With the team also competing for the Bundesliga title and progressing in Europe, talk of multiple trophies has naturally started to gather momentum.
Not surprisingly, Thomas Tuchel was reluctant to join in. Speaking afterward, the Bayern manager praised the performance but avoided making any bold predictions about what the team might achieve by the end of the season.
"It's a good win, nothing more than that right now," he said. "There are still many important matches ahead, and things can change very quickly in football."
One reason for Bayern's confidence is the strength of the squad. Even with a few regular starters given a rest, the team looked comfortable for long stretches of the game. Younger players contributed when called upon, while the more experienced members of the squad ensured the performance never lost its structure.
Defensively, Bayern appears far more settled than it did earlier in the campaign. The back line has developed a stronger understanding, and the team as a whole has become harder to break down.
Going forward, the threat comes from several different areas. Rather than relying on one star player to produce goals, Bayern has seen contributions from attackers, midfielders, and even defenders throughout the season.
For now, the club remains in the hunt on several fronts. Supporters may be looking ahead and imagining what is possible, but inside the dressing room the message seems much simpler: keep winning, and everything else can wait.
A difficult league match awaits this weekend, providing another test of whether Bayern can maintain the momentum that has carried them through recent months.

(CNN) Parents who push their children toward greatness have often been portrayed as controlling or misguided figures on screen, but "Rising Star" takes a different approach. The sports drama places its focus squarely on a determined father whose relentless belief in his daughter's talent becomes the foundation of the story. Anchored by a commanding lead performance from Michael B. Jordan, the film rises above familiar genre formulas even when it occasionally leans on them.
The movie chronicles the early years of track-and-field prodigy Maya Johnson (played by Nia Thompson), following her development under the guidance of her father, Marcus (Jordan). Long before college recruiters and national attention arrived, Marcus had already mapped out a detailed vision for her future, convinced she could become one of the country's elite athletes.
"We're not chasing medals," Marcus repeatedly tells skeptical observers. "We're building a legacy."
That confidence often borders on stubbornness. Marcus spends much of the film challenging coaches, questioning established training methods and insisting that his daughter progress at a pace he believes is right. His determination frequently creates tension with others, including Maya's mother (Regina Hall), who shares responsibility for her daughter's development but worries about the pressure being placed on her.
Director Gina Prince-Bythewood and screenwriter Aaron Covington present Marcus largely through an admiring lens. He is depicted as a tireless advocate who balances multiple jobs, shields his family from negative influences and remains unwavering in his belief that Maya deserves opportunities often denied to athletes from her background.
Even moments that could be interpreted as excessive—early-morning workouts, strict routines and difficult sacrifices—are framed as evidence of his commitment rather than flaws in judgment. As Maya's talent begins attracting national attention, the film increasingly validates Marcus' unconventional methods.
The story becomes somewhat more conventional in its later stages. Predictable conflicts emerge over sponsorship opportunities, coaching decisions and the demands that accompany growing fame. Inspirational montages and swelling music occasionally push the film toward familiar sports-drama territory.
Still, Jordan's performance remains compelling throughout. He captures Marcus' charisma, intensity and unshakable confidence, creating a character whose certainty can be frustrating while remaining understandable. His portrayal gives emotional weight to scenes that might otherwise feel overly familiar.
The film also appears interested in revisiting public perceptions of ambitious sports parents. By emphasizing Marcus' sacrifices and motivations, "Rising Star" argues that his actions stemmed less from personal ambition than from a desire to create opportunities for his daughter.
That perspective shapes nearly every aspect of the narrative. The result is a movie that functions not only as an inspirational sports story but also as a defense of a father whose methods were often questioned by those around him.
Whether audiences fully embrace that interpretation may vary. What is difficult to dispute, however, is the strength of the central performance. Thanks largely to Jordan's work, "Rising Star" succeeds in turning a familiar sports biography into an engaging character study.
"Rising Star" opens nationwide Friday. It is rated PG-13.
